                \item \textbf{Full pipeline}: If pipelining is supported and the pipeline can be kept full, six instructions can be processed at once (in different stages), so the machine can run six times as fast.
                    \bigbreak \noindent 
                    In fact, one instruction will emerge from the pipeline every cycle. Suppose machine speed is 10 ns with full pipeline:
                    \begin{align*}
                        1 \text{cycle}/\text{instr} * 10\; \text{ns}/\text{cycle} = 10\; \text{ns}/\text{instr}
                    .\end{align*}
                    So,
                    \begin{align*}
                        \left(\frac{1}{10} \times 10^{9}\right) \text{inst}/\text{sec} = \left(100 \times 10^{6}\right) \text{inst} / \text{sec}
                    .\end{align*}
            \end{itemize}
            \textbf{Note:} We conventionally use multiples of 3 for the powers of 10, e.g., 106 mips = 106 million instr/sec
        \item \textbf{Steps of different lengths}: Suppose $S_{4}$ takes 2 cycles and $S_{5}$ takes 4 cycles. The total time for an instruction to pass through the pipeline is
            \begin{align*}
                1 + 1 + 1 + 2 + 4 + 1 = 10\; \text{cycles}
            .\end{align*}
            The slowest step (i.e., the step with the maximum time) determines the speed of the pipeline, so one instruction emerges every 4 cycles. In other words, we get 4 cycles/instr instead of 10 cycles/instr with no pipeline.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            At 10 ns/cycle with full pipeline, 
            \begin{align*}
                4 \text{cycle}/\text{instr} \times 10 \text{ns}/\text{cycle} = 40 \text{ns}/\text{instr}
            .\end{align*}
            So,
            \begin{align*}
                \left(\frac{1}{40} \times 10^{9}\right)\; \text{inst} / \text{sec} = \left(25 \times 10^{6}\right)\; \text{inst} / \text{sec}
            .\end{align*}
        \item \textbf{Multiple copies of a step}: Suppose we still have that $S_{5}$ takes 4 cycles per instruction. We could speed up the process by adding a second copy of the circuit for that step.
            Then the slowest step would take 2 cycles/instr, since instructions can alternate going through the left-hand copy or the right-hand copy of S5.
        \item \textbf{two copies of a step, but duplication doesn't double the speed}: Remember that total time is determined by the slowest step, which is not necessarily the duplicated step.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            Suppose S4 takes 3 cycles and S5 takes 4. Duplicating S5 means that S5 effectively takes 2 cycles/sec. But duplicating S5 won't double the instructions per second because S4 is now the slowest step.
            In this case, total time = 11 cycles/instr. The slowest step is 3 cycles/sec.
        \item \textbf{Pipeline stalling}: Stalling means that the pipeline is no longer full because one instruction took more cycles than expected
        \item \textbf{Pipeline flushing}: Flushing the pipeline means that no new instructions can enter the pipeline until the given instruction is finished.
        \item \textbf{Pipeline hazards}: These calculations takes a number of things for granted. First, we have to assume that the architecture supports fetching instructions and data in parallel.
            Second, we assume that the pipeline can be kept filled at all times. This is not always the case. Pipeline hazards arise that cause pipeline conflicts and stalls
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            An instruction pipeline may stall or be flushed for any of the following reasons
            \begin{enumerate}
                \item \textbf{Resource conflicts}: When an instruction needs a resource, such as a register, that a previous instruction still in the pipeline is still using
                \item \textbf{Data dependencies}: When an instruction needs the result of a previous instruction still in the pipeline
                \item \textbf{Conditional branching}: When a branch statement such as "if a then go to b else go to c" occurs in the pipeline, and b or c occur soon after this statement. Neither b nor c can enter the pipeline until the result of the branch is determined, because we do not know whether they will ever be executed at all.
            \end{enumerate}
            When any of these pipeline hazards occurs, the later instruction cannot enter or continue along the pipeline until the conflict is resolved
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            Measures can be taken at the software level as well as at the hardware level to reduce the effects of these hazards, but they cannot be totally eliminated
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            When generating code one statement at a time, it is easy to get redundant instructions like STORE R1, X as the last step of one instruction followed by LOAD R1, X as the first step of the next. An optimizing compiler can remove these.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            An optimizing compiler can also use different registers for operations we may want to execute in parallel
        % \item \textbf{The pipeline bottleneck problem}: Suppose you have $n$ doors labeled $d_{1}, d_{2}, ..., d_{i}, ..., d_{n}$, where each door takes $t_{i}$ time to get through. If each door can handle one person at a time, and people move through each doors $1$ through $n$ sequentially before finishing, how many seconds does it take to see somebody exit door $n$.

    \end{itemize}

    \pagebreak 
    \subsubsection{Instruction set architecture (ISA)}
    \begin{itemize}
        \item \textbf{ISA intro}:
            The instruction set (ISA) of an architecture is the set of instructions that the CPU can handle. The ISA is usually defined by a manual such as the IBM mainframe Principles of Operation. To conform to principles of good hardware and software design, the ISA is usually highly organized. It is not just a random set of instructions.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            A key fact about an ISA is the number of bits per instruction. This can be fixed or variable. A second key fact about an ISA is the basic operating model of the CPU. We will study three models
            \begin{enumerate}
                \item Stack-based
                \item Accumulator-based
                \item Register-based. Also called GPR (general purpose register).
            \end{enumerate}
            Another key fact about an ISA is the set of instruction types that it contains. Instructions can be characterized by
            \begin{itemize}
                \item Number of operands per instruction
                \item Size of operands
                \item Type of operands, e.g., memory or register
            \end{itemize}
        \item \textbf{IBM GPR design}: The IBM mainframe (a GPR design) includes the following types of instructions:
            \begin{itemize}
                \item \textbf{Two-byte instructions (1 or 2 addresses)}:
                    \begin{itemize}
                        \item \textbf{SI (storage-immediate)}: one operand is in memory, the other is given in the instruction (i.e., as a constant)
                        \item \textbf{RR}: register to register
                    \end{itemize}
                \item \textbf{Four-byte instructions (2 or 3 addresses)}:
                    \begin{itemize}
                        \item \textbf{RS}: register to memory
                        \item \textbf{RX}: register to memory, with another register as an index register
                    \end{itemize}
                \item \textbf{Six-byte instructions (3 addresses)}:
                    \begin{itemize}
                        \item \textbf{SS (storage-to-storage)}: multiple operands are memory addresses
                    \end{itemize}
            \end{itemize}
        \item \textbf{Comparing ISAs}: Although there is no way to say that one ISA is "better" than another, some metrics that can be applied to an ISA include:
            \begin{itemize}
                \item Amount of memory required to implement a specific algorithm
                \item Instruction complexity
                \item Average instruction length (in bits)
                \item Total number of instructions in the instruction set
            \end{itemize}
        \item \textbf{Designing an ISA}: Some factors to take into account in designing an ISA include
            \begin{itemize}
                \item Instruction length: short, long, or variable
                \item Number of operands needed
                \item Number of addressable registers
                \item Memory organization: byte- or word addressable
                \item Addressing modes. any or all of direct, indirect or indexed
            \end{itemize}
            ISA and hardware have to be designed together, the factors above are determined by basic features of the CPU
        \item \textbf{CPU operating models}: There are three main options
            \begin{enumerate}
                \item Stack architecture
                \item Accumulator architecture
                \item General purpose register (GPR) architecture
            \end{enumerate}
            In choosing one over the other, the basic tradeoff is simplicity (and cost) of hardware design vs. ease of use
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            As machines become faster and software becomes more sophisticated, these differences become less important, the Assembler can make up for lack of options in the ISA, the compiler usually hides these differences from user
        \item \textbf{Stack architecture}: In a stack architecture, the stack is an implicit operand of every operation, only the top element (or elements) of the stack can be accessed
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            In an accumulator architecture, the accumulator is an implicit operand of every binary operation, the other operand is in memory, creating lots of bus traffic
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            In a general purpose register (GPR) architecture, registers can be used instead of memory
            \begin{itemize}
                \item Faster than accumulator architecture
                \item Efficient implementation for compilers
                \item Results in longer instructions because all operands need to be explicitly specified (no implicit use of stack or accumulator)
            \end{itemize}
        \item \textbf{GPR systems}: GPR systems are very common today. There are three types
            \begin{enumerate}
                \item Memory-memory where two or three operands may be in memory
                \item Register-memory where at least one operand of an instruction must be in a register
                \item Load-store where no operands may be in memory except for the LOAD and STORE instructions
            \end{enumerate}
            The number of operands in an instruction and the number of available registers both affect instruction length
        \item \textbf{IBM mainframe}: IBM mainframe is a prime example of a GPR system. Its design was a savvy business decision in two ways
            \begin{enumerate}
                \item \textbf{Upgrade path}: From the beginning, IBM planned to offer a series of machines with different architectures but the same ISA
                \item \textbf{Features for multiple kinds of users}: Provided decimal for business users, floating point for scientific users, and binary integers for everyone
            \end{enumerate}
        \item \textbf{Infix notation}: Infix notation is the common way we write mathematical expressions, where the operator is placed between two operands.
            \begin{itemize}
                \item Operators appear in the middle (between operands).
                \item It often requires parentheses to clarify order of operations.
                \item It's the standard notation used in everyday math and programming.
            \end{itemize}
            For example, $a+b$ or $(a + b) \cdot (c + d)$.
        \item \textbf{Postfix notation (reverse polish notation)}: Reverse Polish notation is a way of writing expressions where the operator comes after the operands, with no need for parentheses. You write the operands first, then the operator. Operations are evaluated as soon as the operator appears, using the most recent operands.
        \item \textbf{Infix to postfix conversion}:
            \begin{enumerate}
                \item Put parentheses around each binary operation that doesn't already have them. Follow the normal order of operations
                \item Starting at the innermost level, move each binary operator to follow its operands.
                \item Repeat at the next level up of parentheses
                \item Continue until all operators follow their operands.
                \item Remove all the parentheses
            \end{enumerate}
            For example, 
            \begin{align*}
                a \cdot b + c / d &= \left((a \cdot b) + (c/d)\right) \\
                                  &\to \left((ab\cdot ) + (cd /)\right) \\
                                  &\to \left((ab\cdot )(cd /) +\right) \\
                                  &=ab \cdot c d / +
            .\end{align*}
            The principal advantage of postfix notation is that parentheses are not needed
        \item \textbf{Stack machines}: Stack machines use zero- and one-operand instructions.
            \begin{enumerate}
                \item \textbf{LOAD and STORE} instructions require a single operand in memory to indicate where the data is being loaded from or stored to
                \item The \textbf{POP}: operation involves only the top element of the stack
                \item Binary instructions (e.g., \textbf{ADD}, \textbf{MULT}) use the top two items on the stack and place the result on the stack. In our examples, we will use the convention that the top of the stack is the rightmost operand.
            \end{enumerate}
            In a stack ISA, the postfix expression
            \begin{align*}
                z = x\; y\; \times\; w\; u\; \times\; +
            .\end{align*}
            Is implemented as
            \begin{align*}
                &\text{PUSH } x \\
                &\text{PUSH } y \\
                &\text{MULT } \\
                &\text{PUSH } w \\
                &\text{PUSH } u \\
                &\text{MULT } \\
                &\text{ADD } \\
                &\text{STORE } z \\
                &\text{POP}
            .\end{align*}
            When a binary operation is executed:
            \begin{enumerate}
                \item The two operands are popped off the stack.
                \item (*) \textbf{The top one (the last one pushed) is on the right side of the operation}
                \item The operation is executed and the inputs are popped
                \item The result is pushed onto the stack.
            \end{enumerate}
            \textbf{Note:} \texttt{STORE} doesn't pop its input, so you need to.
        \item \textbf{Expressions in an Accumulator machine}: The infix expression
            \begin{align*}
                x \cdot y + w \cdot u
            \end{align*}
            can be calculated like this
            \begin{align*}
                &\text{LOAD } X  \\
                &\text{MULT } Y  \\
                &\text{STORE } \text{ TEMP} \\
                &\text{LOAD } W \\
                &\text{MULT } U  \\
                &\text{STORE } \text{ TEMP2}  \\
                &\text{LOAD } \text{ TEMP} \\
                &\text{ADD } \text{ TEMP2} \\
                &\text{STORE } Z 
            .\end{align*}
            the AC is the left-hand operand of a binary operator.
        \item \textbf{Expressions in a GPR machine}: We can calculate the same expression like
            \begin{align*}
                &\text{LOAD } R1,X  \\
                &\text{MULT } R1,Y  \\
                &\text{LOAD } R2,W  \\
                &\text{MULT } R2,U  \\
                &\text{ADD } R1,R2  \\
                &\text{STORE } Z,R1
            .\end{align*}
            With a three-address machine, we could do
            \begin{align*}
                &\text{MULT } R1,X,Y  \\
                &\text{MULT } R2,W,U  \\
                &\text{ADD } Z,R1,R2
            .\end{align*}
        \item \textbf{Architecture comparison}: Which of these architectures is faster depends on many factors, including:
            \begin{itemize}
                \item Memory access speed
                \item Cache size and speed. A cache is a small amount of fast memory where frequently used data can be stored.
                \item Cycle time
                \item Pipelining. 
            \end{itemize}
            In other words, neither the fact that one machine has shorter instructions nor the fact that another requires fewer instructions to implement a given algorithm is a guarantee of greater speed
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            Again, regardless of hardware manufacturers' ads, cycle time alone is not a determining factor of speed
            \begin{itemize}
                \item A compute-bound program is one where the CPU is busier than I/O, e.g., meteorology.
                \item An I/O-bound program is one where I/O prevents full use of the CPU. Most of our programs are I/Obound, which means that disk access time is another factor determining speed.
            \end{itemize}
    \end{itemize}

    \pagebreak 
    \subsection{Chapter 8: Software}
    \subsubsection{Assemblers}
    \begin{itemize}
        \item \textbf{Intro}: Mnemonic instructions, such as LOAD 104, are easier for humans to write and understand than binary instructions. 
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            The assembler translates instructions that are comprehensible to humans into binary for the CPU to interpret
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            The assembler creates executable software compatible with the operating system and available hardware.
        \item \textbf{Machine code}: When an assembler translates an instruction like \texttt{LOAD $x$}, the assembler produces a binary sequence that contains:
            \begin{enumerate}
                \item \textbf{Opcode}: the instruction identifier (e.g., “LOAD")
                \item \textbf{Operands}: such as the address, register number, or immediate value
                \item \textbf{Possibly other fields}: depending on the CPU (flags, function bits, etc.)
            \end{enumerate}
            That binary sequence is the machine code.
        \item \textbf{Object files}: Assemblers create an \textit{object program file} from mnemonic source code in two passes.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            An object file is a partially compiled file produced by a compiler before linking. It contains machine code, but the code is not yet fully ready to run because external references (functions, variables in other files, libraries, etc.) are still unresolved.
        \item \textbf{Forward / backward references}: Forward references happen when your code refers to something before it has been defined.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            A \textbf{backward reference} is simply when your code refers to something that has already been defined earlier.
        \item \textbf{The two passes}:
            \begin{enumerate}
                \item \textbf{During the first pass}: The assembler assembles as much of the program as it can, while it builds a symbol table that contains memory references for all symbols in the program
                \item \textbf{During the second pass}: The instructions are completed using the values from the symbol table.
            \end{enumerate}
        \item \textbf{The symbol table and the first/second pass}: The \textbf{symbol table} is basically the assembler’s or compiler’s name-tracker.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            It’s a map which records every important name it sees—like labels, variables, and functions—and remembers information about them (such as their address or type).
            \begin{itemize}
                \item When the assembler sees a name (like a label), it writes it into the symbol table along with where it is in the program.
                \item If the program uses a name before it’s defined (a forward reference), the assembler notes it and later comes back to fill in the correct address once the name is finally defined.
                \item When linking multiple object files, the linker uses the symbol tables from each file to match up which names belong to which actual addresses.
            \end{itemize}
            The symbol table is how the compiler/assembler keeps track of what every name means and where it is.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            Two passes are needed to handle forward references. Suppose your program contains \texttt{B $x$}, where $x$ is an address that occurs after the branch statement.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            How can the assembler know the absolute address of $x$, i.e., how can it know how many bytes will occur between the branch statement and the label $x$?
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            During the first pass, when the assembler first encounters the label / symbol $x$, the assembler adds the label $x$ to the symbol table. It puts zeros in the address field of the code being assembled for the branch statement, i.e., it leaves the address unresolved until it finds it. Still in the first pass, once $x$ is found, it updates the symbol table to now map $x$ to its address, since it now knows where $x$ is defined.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            References are resolved during the second pass. By the end of the first pass, the symbol table is fully built. As long as the symbols/labels we use in the program are defined, the symbol table will map that symbol / label to its address. On the second pass, it looks up all unresolved references and replaces the zeros (in the machine code) with the correct address.
        \item \textbf{The linker}: The linker is a tool that takes one or more object files (the partially-compiled outputs from the assembler/compiler) and combines them into a single final program, which is either an executable file, a library, or a module
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            When you compile a program with multiple files, each object file contains
            \begin{itemize}
                \item machine code
                \item missing addresses for external symbols (functions/variables in other files)
                \item a symbol table
            \end{itemize}
            The linker’s job is to:
            \begin{itemize}
                \item Match up all the undefined symbols with their actual definitions.
                \item Fill in all missing addresses in the machine code.
                \item Lay out all the code and data in memory in a single, unified executable.
            \end{itemize}
        \item \textbf{The two passes, example}: Consider the program
            \begin{center}
                \begin{tabularx}{\textwidth}{@{}ll@{}}
                    \toprule
                    \textbf{Address} & \textbf{Instruction} \\
                    \midrule
                    100 & Load $x$ \\[2ex]
                    101 & Add $y$ \\[2ex]
                    102 & Store $z$ \\[2ex]
                    103 & Halt \\[2ex]
                    104 $x$, & DEC $35$ \\[2ex]
                    105 $y,$& DEC $-23$ \\[2ex]
                    106 $z,$& HEX $0000$ \\
                    \bottomrule
                \end{tabularx}
            \end{center}
            During the first pass, we know that LOAD will generate op code ‘1’, but we don’t know the address of $x$ yet. For HALT, we can generate the entire instruction because it has no addresses to resolve.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            When we get to $x$, we can see that its address will be 104. So, at the end of the first pass, we have the symbol table
            \begin{center}
                \begin{tabularx}{\textwidth}{@{}ll@{}}
                    \toprule
                    \textbf{Symbol} & \textbf{Address} \\
                    \midrule
                    $x$ & \textit{unresolved} \\[2ex]
                    $y$ &  \textit{unresolved} \\[2ex]
                    $z$ & \textit{unresolved} \\
                    \bottomrule
                \end{tabularx}
            \end{center}
            and partial machine code
            \begin{center}
                \begin{tabularx}{\textwidth}{@{}X@{}}
                    \toprule
                    \textbf{Machine code} \\
                    \midrule
                    1 $000$ \\[2ex]
                    3 $000$ \\[2ex]
                    2 $000$ \\[2ex]
                    7 $000$ \\
                    \bottomrule
                \end{tabularx}
            \end{center}
            In the second pass, we fill in addresses for $x$, $y$, and $z$.
            \begin{center}
                \begin{tabularx}{\textwidth}{@{}X@{}}
                    \toprule
                    \textbf{Machine code} \\
                    \midrule
                    1 $104$ \\[2ex]
                    3 $105$ \\[2ex]
                    2 $106$ \\[2ex]
                    7 $000$ \\
                    \bottomrule
                \end{tabularx}
            \end{center}
        \item \textbf{Output of assemblers (relocatable binary code)}: The output of most assemblers is a stream of \textbf{relocatable binary code}. 
            In relocatable code, operand addresses are relative to where the operating system chooses to load the program.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            When relocatable code is loaded for execution, special registers provide the base address. Addresses specified within the program are interpreted as offsets from the base address.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            \textbf{Note:} Absolute (nonrelocatable) code is most suitable for device and operating system control programming
        \item \textbf{Absolute code}: Absolute code is machine code that uses fixed, hard-coded memory addresses. Because the addresses are built directly into the code, the program:
            \begin{itemize}
                \item must be placed at a specific location in memory
                \item cannot be moved without breaking the program
                \item has no relocation, no flexibility
            \end{itemize}
            This was common in early computers and simple embedded systems.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            If code is absolute, then
            \begin{itemize}
                \item Jumps contain absolute addresses
                \item Loads/stores contain absolute addresses
                \item The code cannot be moved
                \item The code cannot be relocated
                \item The code cannot be linked with other modules
                \item The code must run at a specific fixed address
            \end{itemize}
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            Absolute code cannot be linked because to link two (or more) object files together, the linker must:
            \begin{itemize}
                \item Combine their code sections into one big program
                \item Reposition code (since each file starts at address 0)
                \item Assign final addresses for all functions and variables
                \item Rewrite instructions so they point to the right final address
            \end{itemize}
            This process is called relocation. But if the code is absolute, the linker cannot change those addresses.
        \item \textbf{Relocatable code}: Relocatable code (also called position-independent code or maintainable code) does NOT contain final memory addresses. Instead, it has symbolic references that the linker or loader fills in later. 
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            the machine code contains:
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            \begin{nasmcode}
            jump SOME_LABEL   <- unresolved reference
            \end{nasmcode}
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            and the linker later patches in the correct address. This makes the program:
            \begin{itemize}
                \item movable
                \item linkable with other object files
                \item loadable anywhere in memory
            \end{itemize}
            \textbf{Note:} If you write a literal address, the assembler encodes it literally, If you reference a symbol (like a label), the assembler inserts:
            \begin{itemize}
                \item An instruction with a placeholder
                \item a relocation entry saying: “The linker must fix this address later."
            \end{itemize}
            Relocatable code means:
            \begin{itemize}
                \item symbols are not bound to final addresses yet
                \item the machine code contains placeholders for symbolic references
                \item the assembler creates relocation entries
                \item the linker or loader will fill in the real addresses later
            \end{itemize}
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            \textbf{Note:} The assembler decides whether to generate relocation entries. 
        \item \textbf{Relocation entry}: A relocation entry is a record that tells the linker: “At this specific location in the machine code, you must insert or adjust the address of this symbol."
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            A relocation entry contains:
            \begin{itemize}
                \item location inside the object file where the placeholder lives
                \item type of relocation (absolute? relative? PC-relative?)
                \item size (how many bytes to rewrite)
                \item which symbol it refers to
                \item addends (if needed for offset calculations)
            \end{itemize}

    \end{itemize}

    \pagebreak 
    \subsubsection{Binding}
    \begin{itemize}
        \item \textbf{Binding}: The process of assigning physical addresses to program variables is called \textbf{binding}.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            Binding can occur at compile time, load time, or run time. Compile time binding gives us absolute code.
        \item \textbf{Compile time binding (absolute code)}: Compile time binding gives us absolute code. In absolute code, a program is always loaded into memory at the same location
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            Absolute code is only used today for programs such as the operating system and network handlers, which can’t be moved around because who would do it? (Imagine the operating system moving itself around while it is running...)
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            Compile-time binding means all symbol addresses are fixed (decided) during compilation/assembly, not delayed until linking or loading.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            If the compiler/assembler knows every address, it must:
            \begin{itemize}
                \item compute and insert final addresses into the machine code
                \item immediately “bind" symbols to their real numeric addresses
            \end{itemize}
            There is nothing left to fix later. If you choose to resolve every address at compile time, the resulting machine code must hard-code those addresses. And that is absolute code. There is no relocation stage, because the compiler has already “locked in" all the addresses.
        \item \textbf{The loader}: The loader is a program / subsystem whose job is to load an executable into memory and prepare it to run.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            The loader is the part of the operating system that:
            \begin{itemize}
                \item Reads the executable file (ELF on Linux, PE on Windows, Mach-O on macOS)
                \item Creates a new process
                \item Allocates virtual memory for code, data, stack, heap
                \item Loads the program’s segments into memory
                \item Performs load-time relocation (if needed)
                \item Links shared libraries (dynamic linking)
                \item Sets up the initial stack and arguments
                \item Jumps to the program’s entry point (main/\_start)
            \end{itemize}
            Then your program begins executing.
        \item \textbf{Load time binding}: Load time binding assigns physical addresses as the program is loaded into memory.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            With load time binding the program can be loaded into different places but it cannot be moved once it is loaded. That is not common today and is included here only for the sake of completeness.
        \item \textbf{Run time binding}: Run time binding requires a base register or equivalent to carry out the address mapping. That is the normal way of loading programs today
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            Runtime binding means the program does NOT know the address of a symbol until the moment that symbol is actually used while the program is running.
        \item \textbf{Link editors (linkers) and static linking}: On most systems, binary instructions must pass through a link editor (or linker) to create an executable module. 
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            Link editors combine multiple binary object files into a single executable file, resolving references to all external symbols. An external symbol is a reference to data or code in another object module
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            \textbf{Note:} This is called \textbf{static linking}.
        \item \textbf{Linkers two passes}: The link editor has two passes, just like the assembler, and for the same reason.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            Suppose module A calls function B which is in a different object file. The first time the linker reads module A, it has not assigned an address to function B yet.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            The first pass reads all the object modules and creates a symbol table containing the names of all external references.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            The second pass reads all the object modules again, assigning addresses from the symbol table to all the external references. It also builds an executable module, which may have a different format from the object modules.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            \fig{.6}{./figures/1040.png}
        \item \textbf{Dynamic linking}: A problem with static linking comes when someone wants to update a function in one of the modules. After updating your function, you need to find every executable it was linked into and relink it.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            A way to solve this problem is dynamic linking. In dynamic linking, any module with a function that can be called is loaded into a dynamic link library (DLL).
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            As the program runs, when an external function is called, the system finds the correct module in the DLL and calls the function
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            The advantage of dynamic linking is that when you want to change a function, you only need to update it in its DLL. You do not need to find all the programs that call it
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            Another advantage of dynamic linking is that executables are smaller, since they don’t contain copies of all the functions they call
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            A disadvantage of dynamic linking is the inverse of the disadvantage of static linking
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            Suppose your program still needs the old version of a function. If the DLL is updated with a new version that is incompatible or simply works differently, your program may no longer work. So you still need to be aware of any library updates




    \end{itemize}

    \pagebreak 
    \subsubsection{Compilers}
    \begin{itemize}
        \item \textbf{Compilers}: A compiler converts a programming language that is comprehensible to humans into binary
        \item \textbf{Assemblers vs compilers}:  In assembly language, there usually is a one-to-one correspondence between a mnemonic instruction and its machine code.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            Compilers allow higher-level constructs such as $X = Y + Z$
        \item \textbf{Higher level assemblers}: Some higher-level assemblers allow constructs such as IF, looping, or even pointer notation. This bridges the difference between a high-level assembler and a low-level language such as C
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            C is called a low-level language because, although it contains constructs such as IF, looping and pointers, there is a much more direct relationship between C code and the compiled binary than with higher-level languages
        \item \textbf{Macros}: In some assemblers, higher-level constructs are implemented as macros.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            In a macro, the programmer writes the higher-level construct, and a pre-pass of the assembler converts the construct to a set of assembler language instructions before the regular assembly process.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            Macros have become deprecated because they create hard-to-find bugs when mixed with compiled code
        \item \textbf{Compiler implementation}: Compilers bridge the gap between the higher level language and the machine’s binary instructions.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            A traditional approach to this translation is a six-phase process. The first three are analysis phases, the last three compiler phases are synthesis phases
            \begin{enumerate}
                \item \textbf{Lexical analysis} extracts tokens, e.g., reserved words and variables.
                \item \textbf{Syntax analysis (parsing)} checks statement construction.
                \item \textbf{Semantic analysis} checks data types and the validity of operators.
                \item \textbf{Intermediate code generation} creates an intermediate representation to facilitate optimization and translation.
                \item \textbf{Optimization} creates assembly code while taking into account architectural features that can make the code efficient.
                \item \textbf{Code generation} creates binary code from the optimized assembly code.
            \end{enumerate}
        \item \textbf{Intermediate code generation: Three address notation}: A traditional form of intermediate code is three-address notation.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            In this form, each instruction has at most three operands, typically a binary operator and an assignment. For example, $Z = X + Y$.
        \item \textbf{Modularity}: Through the use of modularity, compilers can be written for various platforms by rewriting only the last two phases.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            Thus front-ends for multiple languages can generate the same three-address notation. Then different code can be generated for different platforms.
        \item \textbf{More (or fewer) phases}: Compilers may have more or fewer phases. Combining the first two or even the first three phases is very common today.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            These phases may be accomplished via the use of a compiler constructor such as YACC (“yet another compiler constructor"). A compiler constructor builds the phases of a compiler from a description of the language.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            The description is often based on BNF (Backus-Naur form), a format for defining each type of statement similar to those used in elementary programming textbooks.
        \item \textbf{Optimization}: 
            \begin{itemize}
                \item \textbf{Pinhole}: Pinhole optimizers use a sliding window to look at small numbers of consecutive assembler instructions. Redundant sequences such as “STORE X, LOAD X, STORE X" can be removed.
                \item \textbf{Global optimizers}: Global optimizers look at the bigger picture. Perhaps a constant “TAX\_RATE = .03" is defined but never used.
                    \bigbreak \noindent 
                    Even if it is used, perhaps it is not necessary to define storage for the variable. Simply replace the variable by the constant wherever it is used.
            \end{itemize}
            Optimizers need to take function calls into account.
        \item \textbf{Compiler phase figure}
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            \fig{.6}{./figures/1041.png}
        \item \textbf{Interpreters}: Interpreters produce executable code (not really) from source code in real time, one line at a time. This makes interpreted languages slower than compiled languages, but they are convenient for debugging because you can update one line at a time and keep running.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            An interpreter:
            \begin{itemize}
                \item Reads bytecode/AST instructions,
                \item Decodes them one by one,
                \item Executes their behavior using pre-written handler routines,
                \item Never produces native CPU instructions.
            \end{itemize}
            A pre-written handler routine is a small piece of code inside an interpreter that implements the behavior of one specific bytecode or AST operation. It is the mechanism an interpreter uses to simulate program instructions instead of compiling them.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            In other words, it simulates the program using its own execution loop.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            Interpreters do not convert IR into machine code. Instead, each instruction is routed to one of these handlers.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            So execution looks like this:
            \begin{enumerate}
                \item Fetch next bytecode instruction.
                \item Look up its handler (often in a switch or dispatch table).
                \item Jump to that handler code.
                \item Handler performs the operation.
                \item Return to the interpreter loop.               
            \end{enumerate}
            This dispatch overhead is why interpreters are slower.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            It would be nice to have the speed of the compiler and the flexibility of the interpreter at the same time. That is possible now due to faster computers that can recompile modules faster and more sophisticated software designs that can handle on-the-fly software updates, Python is a good example.
        \item \textbf{Python}: Python demonstrates that a language can behave like an interpreter while still doing significant compilation, providing the best of both worlds.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            When you run Python
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            \begin{bashcode}
                python main.py
            \end{bashcode}
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            it seems like the interpreter is just executing your source code line by line, but Python actually compiles your code automatically. 
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            Before execution, CPython (the official Python implementation) compiles your source (.py) into bytecode (.pyc), which lives in \_\_pycache\_\_/. This bytecode is
            \begin{itemize}
                \item Lower-level
                \item Faster to execute
                \item Platform-independent
                \item Not the original source code
            \end{itemize}
            This compilation happens on the fly, so you don’t see it. So Python is not “purely interpreted", it’s a hybrid.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            The Python "interpreter" is a virtual machine, called the Python Virtual Machine (PVM). The PVM
            \begin{itemize}
                \item Reads bytecode instructions
                \item Executes them one at a time
                \item Just like a classic interpreter executes source lines
            \end{itemize}
            This hybrid design is why Python can maintain the interactive benefits of an interpreter while gaining speed from compilation.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            Modern Python implementations even use extra optimizations. This includes
            \begin{itemize}
                \item JIT (Just-In-Time compilation) in PyPy
                \item C-accelerated modules (NumPy, CPython internals)
                \item aggressive caching (.pyc files)
                \item dynamic recompilation of hot code paths
            \end{itemize}
            These make execution much faster than early interpreters.
        \item \textbf{JITs (Just-In-Time Compilers)}: A Just-In-Time (JIT) compiler is a component of a runtime system that compiles program code while the program is executing. Unlike a traditional ahead-of-time (AOT) compiler that produces machine code before execution, a JIT compiler generates optimized machine code on demand during runtime.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            A JIT compiler generates native machine code in memory only, during execution.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            That code:
            \begin{itemize}
                \item lives in RAM,
                \item is used by the CPU immediately,
                \item disappears when the program exits.
            \end{itemize}
            A JIT
            \begin{itemize}
                \item Compiles only the “hot" (frequently executed) sections of the program.
                \item Leaves cold code interpreted.
            \end{itemize}
            The program never becomes a complete native binary.
        \item \textbf{Compilers and interpreters}: Theoretically, both an assembler and a compiler produce binary machine code.
            An interpreter allows code to be executed directly, without compilation. In reality, life has become more complicated than that
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            Compilers have adopted some interpreted features. For example, binary code can be augmented with additional debugging information, allowing interactive debugging
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            Interpreters have adopted some features of compilers. Usually the interpreter produces some sort of intermediate representation (e.g., Java bytecode) to allow efficient execution. People have written compilers, e.g., JIT, for Java bytecode
    \end{itemize}

    \pagebreak 
    \subsubsection{Java case study}
    \begin{itemize}
        \item \textbf{JVM}: Java programs (classes) execute within a virtual machine, the Java Virtual Machine (JVM). This allows the language to run on any platform for which a virtual machine environment has been written.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            Therefore Java is partially compiled, partially interpreted. The output of the compilation process is an assembly-like intermediate code (bytecode) that is interpreted by the JVM.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            The JVM is an operating system in miniature. It loads programs, links them, starts execution threads, manages program resources, and deallocates resources when the programs terminate
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            Because the JVM runs on top of the operating system, its performance cannot match the performance of a traditional compiled language.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            \fig{.6}{./figures/1042.png}
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            JVMs have been written for many processors, including MIPS and Intel. Like a real machine, the JVM bytecode has its own ISA. This ISA was designed to be compatible with the architecture of any machine on which one might want to run Java
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            Extra layers of software often cause a performance hit
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            \fig{.6}{./figures/1043.png}
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            At execution time, a Java Virtual Machine must be running on the host system. It loads and executes the bytecode class file
            \begin{itemize}
                \item While loading the class file, the JVM verifies the integrity of the bytecode.
                \item The loader then performs a number of run-time checks as it places the bytecode in memory.
                \item Finally, the loader invokes the bytecode interpreter. 
            \end{itemize}
        \item \textbf{The bytecode interpreter}:
            \begin{enumerate}
                \item Performs a link edit of the bytecode instructions by asking the loader to supply all referenced classes and system binaries, if they are not already loaded.
                \item Creates and initializes the main stack frame and local variables.
                \item Creates and starts execution thread(s).
                \item Manages heap storage by deallocating unused storage while the threads are executing.
                \item Deallocates resources of terminated threads.
                \item Upon program termination, kills any remaining threads and terminates the JVM
            \end{enumerate}
        Java bytecode is a stack-based language
            \begin{itemize}
                \item Most instructions are zero address instructions
                \item The JVM has four registers that provide access to five regions of main memory
                \item All references to memory are offsets from these registers.
            \end{itemize}
        \item \textbf{Java performance}: Because the JVM loads and executes bytecode rather than executing a binary program, it can't match the performance of a compiled language. This is true even when software like Java’s Just-In-Time (JIT) compiler is used.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            However, Java performance has been steadily increasing over the years. For many applications, the difference is not important, since execution speed is not the limiting factor in an application. Human thinking and business processes are often the limiting factors in computer systems.
        \item \textbf{Advantages of interoperability}: An advantage of Java is that class files can be created and stored on one platform and executed on a different platform
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            This “write once, run-anywhere" paradigm is of enormous benefit for enterprises with disparate and geographically separate systems.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            Java was designed for platform interoperability.  Original slogan: “Write once, run anywhere", took a while to achieve
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            Another advantage of the bytecode system is that other languages can be created that compile into Java bytecodes, Kotlin is one example.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            That is much less work than building a full compiler.
        \item \textbf{Disadvantages of interoperability}: Interoperability has performance cost. Over the years people have started to build native mode compilers for Java (i.e., compiling bytecode instead of interpreting it) to improve performance
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            Interoperability can have a compatibility cost. You need to have correct version of Java Runtime Engine (JRE) on your machine to run a Java program.  In practice, that is largely automatic today.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            Java tries hard to keep new versions of the JRE backwards compatible so that they can run any Java program.






    \end{itemize}

    \pagebreak 
    \unsect{Operating systems}
    \subsection{Overeview and history of OS}
    \begin{itemize}
        \item \textbf{Layers of a computer}
            \begin{enumerate}
                \item \textbf{Application software}
                \item \textbf{Operating system}
                \item \textbf{Hardware}
            \end{enumerate}
        \item \textbf{What is an operating system}: A	software	that	manages	the	computer	hardware. An	intermediary	between	the	user	of	a	computer	and	the	computer	hardware.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            An OS is software
        \item \textbf{Parts of an OS}
            \begin{enumerate}
                \item \textbf{Resource allocator}: 	Decides	between	conflicting	requests	for	efficient	and	fair	resource	use, resources like time and space.
                \item \textbf{Control program}: Controls	execution	of	programs	to	prevent	errors	and	improper	use	of	the	computer	
                \item \textbf{Extended (virtual machine)}: An	environment	in	which	a	user	can	execute	programs	in	a	convenient,	efficient	and	secure	manner.	
                    \bigbreak \noindent 
                    High	usability	for	programmers, high	usability	for	users
            \end{enumerate}
        \item \textbf{An attempt of definition}: The	operating	systems	is	the	most	fundamental	part	of	all	the	system	programs.	It	controls	the	computer’ resources	and	provides	the	base	upon	which	the	application	program	can	be	written	and	the	environment	within	which	the	programs	can	be	executed.	
        \item \textbf{The kernel}: The one program running at all times on the computer.
        \item \textbf{What if there is no operating system?}: This would mean that the application software directly works with computer hardware
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            \textbf{Questions:}
            \begin{enumerate}
                \item Every	piece	of	hardware	is	different	(different	CPU,	different	amount	of	memory,		different	types	of	devices).	Does	every	program	have	to	be	written	to	work	with	all	different	hardware?
                \item Does	every	program	have	access	to	all	hardware?	
                \item Does	a	program	crash	bring	down	the	whole	system?
            \end{enumerate}
        \item \textbf{Contents of a typical operating system}:
            \begin{itemize}
                \item Process	management	
                \item Main-memory	management	
                \item Secondary-storage	management	
                \item File	system		
                \item I/O	system	
                \item Protection/Security	system
            \end{itemize}
        \item \textbf{Why study operating systems?} Knowing	operating	system	helps	you	to	develop	efficient,		effective	and	secure	software.	
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            Helps answer questions about programs like
            \begin{itemize}
                \item Why is runs slow
                \item Why is it not connecting to the web
            \end{itemize}
            OS	itself	is	a	piece	of	software	(of	complexity).	If	you	understand	some	of	the	principles	in	designing	this	software,	such	as	efficiency,	synchronization,	one	day	you	may	find	it	useful	in	the	software	you	design.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            Think	about	the	dilemmas	that	an	OS	faces	every	millisecond
            \begin{itemize}
                \item How	should	a	processor	allocate	its	“attention"	to	perform	all	the	tasks	the	users	asks	of	it,	and	do	so	with	little	overhead?	
                \item When	and	how	should	it	switch	between	different	tasks?		
                \item What	is	the	best	way	to	use	its	limited	memory	resources?				
            \end{itemize}
        \item \textbf{Summary}: An OS is as 
        \begin{itemize}
            \item Software,
            \item Resource allocator,
            \item Virtual machine
        \end{itemize}
        The kernel is the program that runs all the time.
    \item \textbf{The evolution of OS}:
        \begin{itemize}
            \item Non-existent 
            \item Mainframes
            \item PC World / GUI World
            \item Multiprocessor systems
            \item Distributed systems
            \item Special Purpose systems
                \begin{itemize}
                    \item Embedded
                    \item Multimedia
                \end{itemize}
            \item Handheld -- Mobile OS 
        \end{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Mainframes}: Used batch systems, which reduced setup time by batching similar jobs. Automatic job sequencing, automatically transfers control from one job to another.
        \bigbreak \noindent 
        Turn-around time can be large from user standpoint. Lack of protection between two user programs, or between user programs and kernel (monitor) systems.
    \item \textbf{Memory layout of simple batch system}: 
        \bigbreak \noindent 
        \fig{.6}{./figures/1044.png}
    \item \textbf{Multi-programming systems}: Populated by IBM OS / 360. 
        Increases CPU utilization by organizing jobs so that the CPU always has one to execute. Requires special hardware to protect each job against snooping by the other ones.
    \item \textbf{OS features needed for multiprogramming}: 
        \begin{itemize}
            \item \textbf{Memory management} - the system must allocate the memory to several jobs.
            \item \textbf{CPU scheduling} - the system must choose among several jobs ready to run. 
            \item \textbf{Disk storage} 
        \end{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Time sharing systems (70s)}:  A logical extension of multiprogramming, allows users to share the computer simultaneously
        \bigbreak \noindent 
        If the response time is quickly enough, the user had the impression that he or she had direct access to the computer. (concept of “context switch").
        \bigbreak \noindent 
        On-line communication between the user and the system is provided; when the operating system finishes the execution of one command, it seeks the next “control statement" from the user’ s keyboard
        \bigbreak \noindent 
        \textbf{Note:} Unix is a time-sharing system
    \item \textbf{A bit about Unix / Linux}: First edition of UNIX was released 11/03/1971, considered one of the most inspiring and influential pieces of software ever written!
        \bigbreak \noindent 
        1974 Ritchie \& Thompson at Bell Labs published a landmark paper about Unix
        \bigbreak \noindent 
        The first edition of Unix let programmers call 34 different low-level routines built into the operating system. Written in C, evolved from B, as invented by Ritchie while working on Unix. 
        \bigbreak \noindent 
        Nearly all of these system calls are still available—and still heavily used—on modern Unix and Linux systems four decades on.
        \bigbreak \noindent 
        For its time, first--edition Unix provided a remarkably powerful environment for software development. Yet it contained just 4200 lines of code at its heart and occupied a 16 KB of main memory when it ran.
    \item \textbf{Personal computer system}: Traditionally single processor. Computer system dedicated to a single user. Had I / O devices such as keyboards, mice, display screens and printers.
        \bigbreak \noindent 
        Had user convenience and responsiveness.
    \item \textbf{Multiprocessor Systems }:
        Multiprocessor systems with more than one CPU in close communication. Increased throughput; economy of scale; increased reliability.
        \bigbreak \noindent 
        Tightly coupled system - processors share memory and a clock; communication usually takes place through the shared memory.
        \bigbreak \noindent 
        Asymmetric or symmetric multiprocessing.
    \item \textbf{Dual core design}: 
        \bigbreak \noindent 
        \fig{.6}{./figures/1045.png}
    \item \textbf{Clustered Systems}: 
        Clustering allows two or more individual systems to share storage. Provides high availability.
        \begin{itemize}
            \item \textbf{Asymmetric clustering:} one server runs the application while other servers standby.
            \item \textbf{Symmetric clustering:} all $N$ hosts are running the application.
        \end{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Distributed Systems }: Distribute the computation among several physically separate, possibly heterogeneous computer systems that are networked to provide the users with access to the various resources that the system maintains
        \bigbreak \noindent 
        Requires networking infrastructure: Local area networks (LAN) or Wide area networks (WAN). Load balancing for high performance and scalability. Fault tolerance; High reliability;
        \bigbreak \noindent 
        Examples: client-server, peer-to-peer or webbased systems
    \item \textbf{Distributed System: Client-server computing}: 
        \begin{itemize}
            \item \textbf{Compute-server} provides an interface to client to request services (i.e. database)
            \item \textbf{File-server} provides interface for clients to store and retrieve files
        \end{itemize}
        \bigbreak \noindent 
        \fig{.6}{./figures/1046.png}
    \item \textbf{Distributed System: Peer-to-peer computing}:  P2P does not distinguish clients and servers. Instead all nodes are considered peers. May each act as client, server or both 
        \bigbreak \noindent 
        Node must join P2P network
        \begin{itemize}
            \item Registers its service with central lookup service on network, or
            \item Broadcast request for service and respond to requests for service via discovery protocol 
        \end{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Distributed System cont}: Web-based computing:
        \begin{itemize}
            \item Web has become ubiquitous
            \item More devices becoming networked to allow web access
            \item New category of devices to manage web traffic among similar servers: load balancers 
        \end{itemize}
        Cloud computing:
        \begin{itemize}
            \item Computing resources (hardware and software) that are delivered as a service over a network (typically the Internet). 
        \end{itemize}





    \end{itemize}

    \pagebreak 
    \subsection{OS structure and system calls}
    \begin{itemize}
        \item \textbf{The kernel}: The kernel is the core control program of an operating system. Formally, it is the privileged component that manages hardware resources and provides fundamental abstractions to user programs.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            It runs in kernel mode (highest privilege level) and mediates all access to the underlying machine.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            At a systems-theoretic level, the kernel is a resource allocator and control authority. It is responsible for:
            \begin{itemize}
                \item CPU time
                \item Memory
                \item Storage
                \item I/O devices
                \item Network interfaces
            \end{itemize}
            User programs cannot access these directly; they must request services from the kernel.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            The operating system includes the kernel plus user-space utilities, libraries, and system programs. On Linux, the kernel is just the vmlinuz binary.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            So, the kernel is a program.
        \item \textbf{Responsibilities of the kernel}: 
            \begin{itemize}
                \item \textbf{Process management}: The kernel implements the abstraction of a process. The scheduler decides which process executes next.
                \item \textbf{Memory management}: The kernel provides virtual memory.
                    \begin{itemize}
                        \item Creates page tables
                        \item Maps virtual addresses to physical memory
                        \item Enforces protection (user vs kernel pages)
                        \item Handles page faults
                        \item Manages swapping (if enabled)
                    \end{itemize}
                    Without the kernel, processes would compete unsafely for RAM.
                \item \textbf{System calls}: The kernel defines the system call interface.
                \item \textbf{File system management}:
                    \begin{itemize}
                        \item Implements file system structures
                        \item Manages inodes and directories
                        \item Controls disk access
                        \item Maintains buffer caches
                    \end{itemize}
                    Files are abstractions provided by the kernel.
                \item \textbf{Device drivers}:  Hardware devices are controlled via drivers, which typically run in kernel space.
                \item \textbf{Interrupt handling}: Hardware devices signal events via interrupts. The kernel
                    \begin{itemize}
                        \item Receives the interrupt
                        \item Executes an interrupt handler
                        \item Schedules further work if necessary
                    \end{itemize}
            \end{itemize}
        \item \textbf{User space and kernel space}: 
            \begin{itemize}
                \item \textbf{Kernel space}: Kernel space is the privileged execution domain in which the operating system kernel runs.
                \begin{itemize}
                    \item Full access to physical memory
                    \item Direct access to CPU control registers
                    \item Ability to execute privileged instructions
                    \item Unrestricted access to hardware devices
                \end{itemize}
                \item \textbf{User space}: User space is the unprivileged execution domain where application programs run. User processes
                    \begin{itemize}
                        \item Cannot directly access hardware
                        \item Cannot execute privileged CPU instructions
                        \item Cannot access kernel memory
                        \item Are isolated from other processes
                    \end{itemize}
                    They run in a restricted CPU mode. If a user process crashes, the OS can safely terminate it without affecting the rest of the system. U
                    \bigbreak \noindent 
                    \textbf{Note:} User programs must request kernel services via system calls.
                    \begin{itemize}
                        \item User code executes a special instruction (syscall, int 0x80, etc.)
                        \item CPU switches to kernel mode
                        \item Kernel validates arguments
                        \item Kernel performs operation
                        \item CPU returns to user mode
                    \end{itemize}
                    This is called a controlled privilege escalation. Without separation, any application could:
                    \begin{itemize}
                        \item Overwrite kernel memory
                        \item Access other processes’ memory
                        \item Directly manipulate hardware
                        \item This would destroy system integrity.
                    \end{itemize}
            \end{itemize}
            At any instant, a CPU core is in one of two modes:
            \begin{itemize}
                \item \textbf{User mode}:
                \item \textbf{Kernel mode}:
            \end{itemize}
            The kernel runs only when the processor is in kernel mode. When the processor is executing user code, the kernel is not actively executing instructions - but it still “exists" in memory and retains control authority.
        \item \textbf{Traps}: In operating systems theory, a trap is a controlled transfer of execution from user mode to kernel mode caused by an instruction or an exceptional condition. It is a synchronous control transfer initiated by the currently executing instruction stream. Traps are fundamental to protection, system calls, and fault handling.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            Formally, A trap is a processor exception that transfers control to a predefined handler in the kernel, triggered by the current instruction.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            Traps serve three primary purposes:
            \begin{enumerate}
                \item System calls
                \item Fault handling
                \item Debugging support
            \end{enumerate}
            Without traps, user programs could not safely request privileged services.
        \item \textbf{OS Structures}: An operating system provides the environment within which programs are executed. We view an operating systems from several vantage points:
            \begin{itemize}
                \item One view focuses on the services that the system provides.
                \item Another on the interface that it makes available to users and programmers.
                \item A third on its components and their interconnections.
            \end{itemize}
        \item \textbf{OS services}: An operating systems provides an environment for the execution of programs. Makes certain services available to programs and to the users of those programs. These services differ from one operating system to another, but we can identify common classes.
            \begin{itemize}
                \item \textbf{User interface}: Almost all operating systems have a UI. Most commonly in the form of a GUI. In the GUI the interface is a window system with a mouse that serves as a pointing device to direct I/O, choose from menus, and make selections and a keyboard to enter text. Another option is a CLI, which uses text commands and a method for entering them (say, a keyboard). Some systems provide multiple of these variations.
                \item \textbf{Program execution}: The system must be able to load a program into memory and run the program. The program must be able to end its execution, either normally or abnormally
                \item \textbf{I/O operations}: A running program may require I/O, which may involve a file or an I/O device. For specific devices, special functions may be desired like reading from a network interface or writing to a file system. For efficiency and protection, users cannot control I/O devices directly, the operating system must provide a means to do I/O.
                \item \textbf{File-system manipulation}: The file system is of particular interest. Programs need to read and write files and directories. They also need to create and delete them by name, search for a given file, and list file information. Finally, some operating systems include permissions management to allow or deny access to files or directories based on file ownership. Many operating systems provide a variety of file systems.
                \item \textbf{Communications}: one process may need to exchange information with other processes. Such communication may occur between processes that are executing on the same computer or between processes that are executing on different computer systems tied together by a network. Communication may be implemented via shared memory, in which two or more processes read and write to a shared section of memory, or message passing, in which packets of information in predefined formats are moved between processes by the OS.
                \item \textbf{Error detection}: The OS needs to be detecting and correcting errors constantly. Errors may occur in the CPU and memory hardware, in I/O devices, and in the user program. For each error type, the OS needs to take appropriate action. Sometimes, it may have no choice but to halt the system. Other times, it may terminate the error-causing process or return an error code to a process for the process to detect and possibly correct.
                \item \textbf{Resource allocation}: When there are multiple processes running at the same time, resources must be allocated to each of them. The OS manages many types of resources. Some (CPU cycles, main memory, file storage) may have special allocation code, whereas others (such as I/O devices) may have much more general request and release code. For instance, in determining how best to use the CPU, operating systems have CPU-scheduling routines that take into account the speed of the CPU, the process that must be executed, the number of processing cores, and other factors.
                \item \textbf{Logging}: We want to keep track of which programs use how much and what kinds of computer resources. May be used for accounting (so the user can be billed) or simply for usage statistics.
                \item \textbf{Protection and security}: The owners of information stored in a multiuser or networked computer system may want to control use of that information. When several Processes execute concurrently, it should not be possible for one process to interfere with the others or with the operating system itself. Security of the system from outsiders is also important. It starts with user authentication via a password, and extends to defending external I/O devices, including network adapters, from invalid access attempts and recording all such connections.
            \end{itemize}
        \item \textbf{Interfaces}:
            \begin{itemize}
                \item \textbf{CLI}: Most OS’s treat the CLI as a special program that is running when a process is initiated or when a user first logs on (on interactive systems). On systems with multiple CLI’s to choose from, the interpreters are known as shells. The main function of the command interpreter is to get and execute the next user-specified command.
                    \bigbreak \noindent 
                    The CLI can be implemented in two general ways.
                    \begin{enumerate}
                        \item The command interpreter itself contains the code to execute the command. For example, the command to delete a file may cause the command interpreter to jump to a section of its code that sets up the parameters and makes the appropriate system calls. In this case, the number of commands that the interpreter offers determines the size of the interpreter, since each command requires its own implementing code.
                        \item Another approach - used by UNIX (among other OS’s) - implements most commands through system programs. In this case, the CLI does not understand the command, it merely uses the command to identify the file to be loaded into memory and executed.
                    \end{enumerate}
                \item \textbf{GUI}:  A user friendly graphical user interface. Rather than entering commands directly via a CLI, users employ a mouse based window and menu system.
            \end{itemize}
        \item \textbf{System calls}: Provides an interface to the services made available by an OS. These calls are generally available as functions written in C and C++. Some low-level tasks may have to be written using assembly instructions.
        \item \textbf{APIs}: Application developers typically write programs that rely on an application programming interface (API). An API defines the set of functions, types, and constants that are available to a programmer, along with the required parameters, return values, and semantic guarantees associated with their use.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            An API is a specification rather than an implementation. In many cases, an API is made available to programmers through a library, which provides the concrete implementation of the API’s functions.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            A well-known example of an operating system API is the Windows API, which provides applications with access to operating system services.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            In the context of operating systems, API functions often act as wrappers around lower-level system calls, performing tasks such as parameter validation and setup before transferring control to the kernel. For example, the Windows function \texttt{CreateProcess()} ultimately invokes the internal system call \texttt{NTCreateProcess()}.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            On UNIX and Linux systems, system calls are commonly accessed through the C API provided by the standard C library, libc, which exposes user-level functions that invoke the corresponding kernel services.
        \item \textbf{System call interface}:  An important factor in handling system calls is the run-time environment (RTE) - the full suite of software needed to execute applications written in a given programming language.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            The RTE provides a system call interface that serves as the link to system calls made available by the OS. The system call interface intercepts function calls in the API and invokes the necessary system calls within the OS. A number is typically associated with each system call. The system-call interface maintains a table indexed according to these numbers. The system-call interface invokes the intended system call in the OS kernel and returns the status of the system call.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            \textbf{Note:} Most of the details of the OS-system interface are hidden from the programmer by the API and are managed by the RTE.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            \fig{1}{./figures/1053.png}
        \item \textbf{Types of syscalls}: 
            \begin{itemize}
                \item \textbf{Process control}: 
                    \begin{itemize}
                        \item create process, terminate process
                        \item load, execute
                        \item get process attributes, set process attributes
                        \item wait event, signal event
                        \item allocate and free memory
                \end{itemize}
                \item \textbf{File management}: 
                    \begin{itemize}
                        \item create file, delete file
                        \item open, close
                        \item read, write, reposition get file attributes, set file attributes
                    \end{itemize}
                \item \textbf{Device management}: 
                    \begin{itemize}
                        \item request device, release device
                        \item read, write, reposition
                        \item get device attributes, set device attributes
                        \item logically attach or detach devices
                    \end{itemize}
                \item \textbf{Information maintenance}: 
                    \begin{itemize}
                        \item get time or date, set time or date
                        \item get system data, set system data
                        \item get process, file, or device attributes
                        \item set process, file, or device attributes
                    \end{itemize}
                \item \textbf{Communications}: 
                    \begin{itemize}
                        \item create, delete communication connection
                        \item send, receive messages
                        \item transfer status information
                        \item attach or detach remote devices
                    \end{itemize}
                \item \textbf{Protection}: 
                    \begin{itemize}
                        \item get file permissions
                        \item set file permissions
                    \end{itemize}
            \end{itemize}
        \item \textbf{Process control}: A running program needs to be able to halt its execution normally (end()) or abnormally (abort()).
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            If a system call is made to terminate the currently running program abnormally, or if the program runs into a problem and causes an error trap, a dump of memory is sometimes taken and an error message generated.
