The dump is written to a special log file on disk. This may be examined by the debugger.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            If the program discovers an error in its input and wants to terminate abnormally, it may also want to define an error level. More severe errors can be indicated by a higher error level. It is then possible to combine normal and abnormal termination by defining a normal termination as error level 0.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            A process executing one program may want to load() or execute() another program. An interesting question is related to where to return control when the loaded program terminates. This question is related to whether the existing program is lost, saved, or allowed to continue execution concurrently with the new program. If control returns to the existing program when the new program terminates, we must save the memory image of the existing program; thus, we have effectively created a mechanism for one program to call another program. If both programs continue concurrently, we have created a new process to be multiprogrammed. Often, there is a system call specifically for this purpose. (create\_process).
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            If we create a new process, or set of processes, we should be able to control its execution.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            This control requires the ability to determine and reset the attributes of a process, including its priority, its max allowable execution time, and so on. (\texttt{get\_process\_attributes()} and \texttt{set\_process\_attributes()}).
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            We may also want to terminate a process that we created (\texttt{terminate\_process()}).
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            We may need to wait for a process to finish their execution. We may want to wait for a certain amount of time to pass (\texttt{wait\_time()})
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            More probably, we will want to wait for a specific event to occur (wait\_event()). The process should then signal when that event has occurred (\texttt{signal\_event()}).
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            Often, two or more processes share data. To ensure integrity of the data, operating systems provide a system call allowing a process to lock shared data. Then, no other process can access the data until the lock is released. Typically, such system calls include \texttt{acquire\_lock()} and \texttt{release\_lock()}.
        \item \textbf{File management}: We first need to be able to create() and delete() files. Either system call requires the name of the file and perhaps some of the file’s attributes. Once the file is created, we need to open() it and to use it. We may also read(), write(), or reposition() (rewind or skip to the end of the file, for example). Finally, we need to close() the file.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            We may need these same sets of operations for directories if we have a directory structure for organizing files in the file system. 
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            In addition, for either files or directories, we need to be able to determine the values of various attributes and perhaps to set them if necessary. File attributes include the file name, file type, protection codes, accounting information, and so on. At least two system calls, get\_file\_attributes() and set\_file\_attributes(), are required for this function. Some operating systems provide many more calls such as calls for file move() and copy()
        \item \textbf{Device management}: A process may need several resources to execute - main memory, disk drives, access to files, and so on. If the resources are available, they can be granted and control can be returned to the user process. Otherwise, the process will have to wait until sufficient resources are available. The various resources controlled by the operating system can be thought of as devices. Some of these are physical devices (disk drives for example). Others are abstract or virtual devices (files for example).
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            A system with multiple users may require us to first request() a device, to ensure the exclusive use of it. After we are finished with the device, we release() it
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            Other operating systems allow unmanaged access to devices. The hazard then is the potential for device contention and perhaps deadlock. Once the device has been requested (and allocated to us), we can read(), write(), and possibly reposition() the device, just as we can with files.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            The similarity to I/O devices and files is so great that many operating systems, including UNIX, merge the two into a combined file-device structure. In this case, a set of system calls is used on both files and devices. Sometimes, I/O devices are identified by special file names, directory placement, or file attributes.
        \item \textbf{Information Maintenance}: Many system calls exist simply for the purpose of transferring information between the user program and the OS. Most systems have a system call to return the current time() and date().
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            Other system calls may return information about the system, such as the version number of the operating system, the amount of free memory, etc. Another set of system calls is helpful in debugging a program. Many systems provide system calls to dump() memory, useful for debugging.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            The programs trace, which is available on Linux, lists each system call as it is executed
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            Even microprocessors provide a CPU mode, known as a single step, in which a trap is executed by the CPU after every instruction. This trap is usually caught by the debugger.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            The OS keeps information about all processes, and system calls are used to access this information. Generally, calls are also used to get and set the process information (get\_process\_attributes() and set\_process\_attributes()).
        \item \textbf{Communication}: Two common models of interprocess communication
            \begin{enumerate}
                \item \textbf{message-passing model}: In the message-passing model the communicating processes exchange messages with one another to transfer information. Messages shared either directly or indirectly through a common mailbox. Before communication, a connection must be opened. The name of the other communicator must be known. Each computer network has a host name by which it is commonly known. A host also has a network identifier, such as an IP address.
                    \bigbreak \noindent 
                    Each process has a process name, and this name is translated into an identifier by which the OS can refer to the process. The get\_hostid() and get\_processid() system calls can do this translation. The identifiers are then passed to the general purpose open() and close() calls provided by the file system or open\_connection() and close\_connection() system calls, depending on the systems model of communication.
                    \bigbreak \noindent 
                    The recipient process usually must give its permission for communication to take place with an accept\_connection() call. Most processes that will be receiving connections are special-purpose daemons. They execute a wait\_for\_connection() call and are awakened when a connection is made. The source of the communication, the client, and the receiving daemon, the server, then exchange messages by using read\_message() and write\_message() system calls. The close\_connection() call terminates the communication.
                \item \textbf{shared-memory model}: In the shared-memory model, processes use shared\_memory\_create() and shared\_memory\_attach() system calls to create and gain access to regions of memory owned by other processes.
                    \bigbreak \noindent 
                    Normally, the operating system tries to prevent one process from accessing another process’s memory. Shared memory requires that two or more processes agree to remove this restriction. They can exchange information by reading and writing data in the shared areas. The form of data is determined by the processes and is not under the operating system’s control. The processes are also responsible for ensuring that they are not writing to the same location simultaneously.
            \end{enumerate}
            It is common for both the message-passing, and shared-memory model to be implemented by the operating system.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            Message passing is useful for exchanging smaller amounts of data, because no conflicts need be avoided. It is also easier to implement
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            Shared memory allows maximum speed and convenience of communication, since it can be done at memory transfer speeds
        \item \textbf{Protection}: Protection provides a mechanism for controlling access to the resources provided by a computer system. Historically only a concern with multiprogrammed systems with several users.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            With the advent of networking and the Internet, all computer systems, from servers to mobile devices must be concerned with protection.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            set\_permission() and get\_permission() manipulate the permission settings of resources such as files and disks. The allow\_user() and deny\_user() system calls specify whether particular users can or cannot be allowed access to certain resources.
        \item \textbf{Logical computer hierarchy}: At the lowest level is hardware, next is the operating system, then the system services, and finally the application programs.
        \item \textbf{System services}: System services, also known as system utilities, provide a convenient environment for program development and execution. Some are simply user interfaces to system calls. Others are considerably more complex.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            Divided into these categories:
            \begin{itemize}
                \item \textbf{File management}: These programs create, delete, copy, rename, print, list and generally access and manipulate files and directories.
                \item \textbf{Status information}: Some programs simply ask the system for the date,time, amount of available memory or disk space, number of users. Others are more complex, providing detailed performance, logging, and debugging information. Typically, these programs format and print the output to the terminal or other output devices or files or display it in a window of the GUI.
                \item \textbf{File modification}: Several text editors may be available to create and modify the content of files stored on disk or other storage devices. May also be special commands to search contents of files or perform transformations of the text.
                \item \textbf{Programming-language support}: Compilers, assemblers, debuggers, and interpreters for common programming languages are often provided with the operating system.
                \item \textbf{Program loading and execution}: Once a program is assembled or compiled, it must be loaded into memory to be executed. The system may provide absolute loaders, relocatable loaders, linkage editors, and overlay loaders. Debugging systems for either higher-level languages or machine languages are needed as well.
                \item \textbf{Communications}: These programs provide the mechanism for creating virtual connections among processes, users, and computer systems. They allow users to send messages to one another’s screens, to browse web pages, to send e-mail messages, to log in remotely or to transfer files from one machine to another.
                \item \textbf{Background services}: All general-purpose systems have methods for launching certain system-program processes at boot time. Some of these processes terminate after completing their tasks, while others continue to run until the system is halted. Constantly running system-program processes are known as services, subsystems, or daemons.
            \end{itemize}
            The view of the operating system seen by most users is defined by the application and system programs, rather than the actual system calls.
        \item \textbf{Linkers and Loaders}: Usually, a program resides on disk as a binary executable file. To run on the CPU, the program must be brought into memory and placed in the context of a file.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            Source files are compiled into object files that are designed to be loaded into any physical memory location, a format known as an \textbf{relocatable object file}.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            The linker combines these relocatable object files into a single binary executable file. During the linking phase, other object files or libraries may be included as well, such as the standard C or math library
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            A loader is used to load the binary executable file into memory, where it is eligible to run on a CPU core.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            When a program is executed, the shell first creates a new process to run the program using the fork() system call. The shell then invokes the loader with the exec() system call, passing exec() then name of the executable file. The loader then loads the specified program into memory using the address space of the newly created process.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            Thus far, we assume all libraries are linked into the executable file and loaded into memory. Most systems however, allow a program to dynamically link libraries as the program is loaded. Windows, for instance, supports dynamically linked libraries (DLLs). DLLs benefit is that it avoids linking and loading libraries that may end up not being used into an executable file. Instead, the library is conditionally linked and is loaded if it is required during program run time.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            Object files and executable files typically have standard formats that include the compiled machine code and a symbol table containing metadata about functions and variables that are referenced in the program. For UNIX and LINUX systems, this format is known as ELF (Executable and Linkable Format). One piece of information in the ELF file for executable files is the programs entry point - the first instruction to be executed when the program runs
        \item \textbf{Applications are Operating-System Specific}: Fundamentally, applications compiled on one operating system are not executable on other operating systems.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            Each OS provides a unique set of system calls, and system calls are part of the set of services provided by the operating systems for use by applications.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            An application can be made available to run on multiple operating systems in one of three ways:
            \begin{enumerate}
                \item The application is written in an interpreted language that has an interpreter available for multiple operating systems. It reads each line of the program, executes equivalent instructions on the native instruction set, and calls native operating system calls. The interpreter provides only a subset of each operating system’s features possibly limiting the feature sets of the applications.
                \item The application was written in a language that includes a virtual machine containing the running application. The VM is part of the language’s full RTE. Java for example.
                \item The application developer can use a standard language or API in which the compiler generates binaries in a machine and operating-system specific language. The application must be ported to each operating system on which it will run.
            \end{enumerate}
            In theory, these three approaches seemingly provide simple solutions for developing applications that can run across different operating systems. However, the general lack of mobility has several causes.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            At the application level, the libraries provided with the operating system contain APIs to provide features like GUI interfaces, and an application designed to call one set of APIs will not work on an operating system that does not provide those APIs.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            Other challenges exist at lower levels in the system.
            \begin{itemize}
                \item Each OS has a binary format for applications that dictates the layout of the header, instructions, and variables. Those components need to be at certain locations in specified structures within an executable file so the OS can open the file and load the application for execution.
                \item CPUs have varying instruction sets, and only applications containing the appropriate instructions can execute correctly.
                \item Operating systems provide system calls that allow applications to request various activities, such as creating files and opening network connections. Those system calls vary among operating systems in many respects.
            \end{itemize}
        \item \textbf{Application binary interface (ABI)}: An application binary interface (ABI) is used to define how different components of binary code can interface for a given operating system on a given architecture.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            An ABI specifies low-level details, including address width, methods of passing parameters to system calls, the organization of the run-time stack, the binary format of system libraries, and the size of data types, to name a few. Typically, an ABI is specified for a given architecture. Thus, an ABI is the architecture-level equivalent of an API.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            If a binary executable file has been compiled and linked according to a particular ABI, it should be able to run on different systems that support that ABI.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            Because a particular ABI is defined for a certain operating system running on a given architecture, ABIs do little to provide cross-platform compatibility
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            In summary, unless an interpreter, RTE, or binary executable file is written for and compiled on a specific operating system on a specific CPU type, the application will fail to run.
        \item \textbf{OS design goals}: First problem in designing a system is to define goals and specifications. At the highest level, the design of the system will be affected by the choice of hardware and the type of the system: traditional desktop/laptop, mobile, distributed
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            The requirements can be divided into two basic groups:
            \begin{enumerate}
                \item \textbf{User goals}: 
                \item \textbf{System goals}: 
            \end{enumerate}
            \textbf{Important principle}: separation of policy from mechanism. Mechanism determines \textbf{how} to do something, policy determines \textbf{what} will be done. The separation of mechanism and policy are important for flexibility, policies are likely to change across places or over time. In the worst case, each change in the policy would result in a change in the underlying mechanism.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            Once an operating system is designed, it must be implemented. Early operating systems were written in Assembly languages. Now, most are written in higher-level languages such as C or C++, with small amounts of the system written in assembly language.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            More than one higher-level language is often used. The lowest levels of the kernel might be written in assembly language and C. Higher-level routines might be written in C and C++, and system libraries might be written in C++ or even higher level languages.
        \item \textbf{OS structure}: A system as large and complex as a modern operating system must be engineered carefully if it is to function properly and be modified easily
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            Partition a task into smaller components, or modules, rather than have one single system. Each module should be a well-defined portion of the system.
        \item \textbf{Monolithic Structure}: The simplest structure is no structure at all. Place all of the functionality of the kernel into a single, static binary file that runs in a single address space. 
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            The original UNIX operating system - consists of two separable parts: the kernel and the system programs. The kernel is further separated into a series of interfaces and device drivers
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            The kernel provides the file system, CPU scheduling, memory management, and other operating system functions through system calls. That is an enormous amount of functionality to be combined into one single address space.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            The Linux OS is based on UNIX and is structured similarly. Applications typically use the glibc standard C library when communicating with the system call interface to the kernel
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            The Linux kernel is monolithic in that it runs entirely in kernel mode in a single address space, but it does have a modular design that allows the kernel to be modified during run time. The monolithic structure does well in speed and efficiency.
        \item \textbf{Layered approach}: The monolithic structure is known as a tightly couped system because changes to one part of the system can have wide-ranging effects on other parts. We could design a loosely coupled system. Such a system is divided into seperate, smaller components that have specific and limited functionality. All these components together comprise the kernel. The advantage is that changes in one component affect only that component.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            A system can be made modular in many ways. One method is the layered approach, in which the OS is broken into a number of layers (levels). The bottom layer (layer 0) is the hardware; the highest layer (layer) $N$ is the user interface.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            An operating-system layer is an implementation of an abstract object made up of data and the operations that can manipulate those data. A typical operating system layer-say, layer M-consists of data structures and a set of functions that can be invoked by higher-level layers. Layer $M$, in turn, can invoke operations on lower-level layers
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            Main advantage of the layered approach is simplicity of construction and debugging. The layers are selected so that each uses functions and services of only lower-level layers. If an error is found on a layer, the error must be on that layer. Because the layers below are already debugged.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            Layered systems have been successfully used in computer networks (such as TPC/IP) and web applications. But few operating systems use a pure layered approach.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            High overhead and hard to define functionality of each layer.
        \item \textbf{Micro-kernels}: This method structures the operating system by removing all nonessential components from the kernel and implementing them as user-level programs that reside in separate address space. The result is a smaller kernel. There is little consensus regarding which services should remain in the kernel and which should be implemented in user space
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            Typically, microkernels provide minimal process and memory management, in addition to a communications facility. The main function of the micro kernel is to provide communication between the client program and the various services that are also running in user space. Communication is provided through message passing.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            For example, if the client program wants to access a file, it must interact with the file server. The client program and file server never interact directly. Rather, they communicate indirectly by exchanging messages with the microkernel.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            This concept makes extending the OS easier. All new services are added to the user space and do not require modifying the kernel.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            \textit{Darwin} is the kernel for the macOS and iOS operating system. It is a microkernel. Darwin, in fact, consists of two kernels, one of which is the Mach microkernel.
        \item \textbf{Modules}: Perhaps the best current methodology for operating system design involves using loadable kernel modules (LKMs). Here, the kernel has a set of core components and can link in additional services via modules, either at boot time or during run time. This type of design is common in modern implementations of UNIX, such as Linux, macOS, and Solaris, as well as Windows.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            The kernel provides core services, while other services are implemented dynamically, as the kernel is running. Linking services dynamically is perferable to adding new features directly to the kernel, which would require recompilation of the kernel. For example, we might build CPU scheduling and memory management algorithms directly into the kernel and then add support for different file systems by way of loadable modules.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            The overall result resembles a layered system in that each kernel section has defined, protected interfaces; but it is more flexible than a layered system, because any module can call any other module.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            Also similar to the microkernel approach in that the primary module has only core functions and knowledge of how to load and communicate with other modules.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            \textbf{Note:} Linux uses loadable kernel modules.
        \item \textbf{Hybrid systems}: Very few operating systems adopt a single, strictly defined structure. Instead they combine different structures. Linux is monolithic. However it is also modular.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            Windows is largely monolithic, but it retains some behavior typical of microkernel systems, including providing support for seperate subsystems that run as user-mode processes.
        \item \textbf{Building and Booting an Operating System}: It is possible to design, code, and implement an operating system specifically for one specific machine configuration. More commonly, operating systems are designed to run on any of a class of machines with a variety of peripheral configurations.
        \item \textbf{Operating System Generation}: If you are generating (or building) an operating system from scratch, you must follow these steps:
            \begin{enumerate}
                \item Write the operating system source code (or obtain previously written source code).
                \item Configure the operating system for the system on which it will run.
                \item Compile the operating system.
                \item Install the operating system.
                \item Boot the computer and its new operating system
            \end{enumerate}
            Configuring the system involves specifying which features will be included. Typically, parameters describing how the system is configured is stored in a configuration file of some type. Once the file is created it can be used in several ways.
            \begin{enumerate}
                \item A system admin can use it to modify a copy of the operating system source code. Then the opearting system is completely compiled (system build). Data declarations, initializations, and constants, along with compilation, produce an output-object version of the operating system that is tailored to the system described in the configuration file.
                \item The system description can lead to the selection of precompiled object modules from an existing library. These modules are linked together to form the generated operating system.
                \item It is possible to construct a system that is completely modular. Here, selection occurs at execution time rather than at compile or link time. System generation involves simply settting the parameters that describe the system configuration.
            \end{enumerate}
            For embedded systems, it is not uncommon to adopt the first approach. Modern operating systems that support desktop and laptop computers as well as mobile devices have adopted the second approach. The OS is still generated for a specific hardware configuration but the use of techniques such as loadable kernel modules provides modular support for dynamic changes to the system.
        \item \textbf{Building linux from scratch.}: 
            \begin{enumerate}
                \item Download the Linux source code.
                \item Configure the kernel using make menuconfig. This generates the .config configuration file.
                \item Compile the kernel using make. The make command compiles the kernel based on the configuration parameters in the .config file, producing the vmlinuz, which is the kernel image.
                \item Compile the kernel modules using the make modules command. Module compilation depends on the configuration parameters specified in the .config file.
                \item Use the command make modules\_install to install the kernel modules into vmlinuz.
                \item Install the new kernel on the system using make install.
            \end{enumerate}
        \item \textbf{System Boot}: After an operating system is generated, it must be made available for use by the hardware. But how does the hardware know where the kernel is or how to load the kernel?
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            The process of starting a computer by loading the kernel is known as \textbf{booting the system}.
            \begin{itemize}
                \item A small piece of code known as the boot loader locates the kernel.
                \item The kernel is loaded into memory and started.
                \item The root file system is mounted.
            \end{itemize}
        \item \textbf{BIOS boot process}: When the computer is powered on, a small boot loader located in nonvolatile firmware known as BIOS is run. This initial boot loader usually does nothing more than load a second bootloader, which is located at a fixed disk location called the boot block.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            The program in the boot block may be sophisticated enough to load the entire operating system into memory and begin its execution. More typically, it is simple code (as it must fit in a single disk block) and knows only the address on disk and the length of the remainder of the boot loader.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
        \item \textbf{UEFI}: UEFI has several advantages over BIOS, including better support for 64-bit systems and larger disks. Perhaps the greatest advantage is that UEFI is a single, complete boot manager and therefore is faster than the multistage BIOS boot process.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            Whether booting from BIOS or UEFI, the boot loader can perform a variety of tasks. In addition to loading the file containing the kernel program into memory, it also runs diagnostics to determine the state of the machine. If the diagnostics pass, the program can continue with the booting steps.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            The bootloader can also initialize all aspects of the system, from CPU registers to device controllers and the contents of main memory. 
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            Sooner or later, it starts the operating system and mounts the root file system. At this point the system is said to be running.
        \item \textbf{GRUB}: Grub is an open-source bootloader for Linux and UNIX systems. Boot parameters for the system are set in a GRUB configuration file, which is loaded at startup.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            GRUB allows changes to be made at boot time, including modifying kernel parameters and even selecting among different kernels that can be booted. To save space and decrease boot time, the Linux kernel image is a compressed file that is extracted after it is loaded into memory.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            \begin{itemize}
                \item Reading a smaller compressed file from disk is faster than reading a large uncompressed file.
                \item Decompression in RAM is very fast relative to disk access time.
            \end{itemize}
            \textbf{Note:} The file must always be read from storage before it can be placed into memory. “Loaded into memory" necessarily implies that it has been read. You can read the bytes of a compressed file exactly like any other file. However, you cannot directly interpret its original contents without decompressing it.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            During the boot process the boot loader typically creates a temporary RAM file system, known as initramfs. Disk I/O is typically much slower than RAM operations and CPU decompression. This file system contains necessary drivers and kernel modules that must be installed to support the real root file system (which is not in main memory).
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            \textbf{Note:} A RAM file system is a file system whose storage medium is main memory (RAM) instead of persistent storage (SSD, HDD, etc.). It behaves like a normal file system from the user’s perspective, but all data resides in volatile memory.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            Once the kernel has started and the necessary drivers are installed, the kernel switches the root file system from the temporary RAM location to the appropriate root file system. Finally linux creates the systemd process, the initial process of the system, and then starts other services.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            \begin{bashcode}
                BOOT_IMAGE=/boot/vmlinuz-4.4.0-59-generic
                root=UUID=5f2e2232...
            \end{bashcode}
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            BOOT\_IMAGE is the name of the kernel image to be loaded into memory, and root specifies a unique identifier of the root file system.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            initramfs exists to solve this fundamental constraint:
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            \begin{center}
                You need drivers to mount the root filesystem.
            \end{center}
            But,
            \begin{center}
                Those drivers may live on the root filesystem 
            \end{center}
            \textbf{Note:} The temporary filesystem does not fetch drivers from the real root filesystem, the required drivers are already packaged inside the initramfs image before boot even begins.
            \begin{itemize}
                \item The bootloader loads:
                    \begin{itemize}
                        \item The kernel
                        \item The initramfs image
                    \end{itemize}
                \item The kernel mounts initramfs as the temporary root.
                \item The /init script runs.
                \item It loads the included modules from RAM.
                \item Once storage drivers are active, the real root filesystem can be mounted.
            \end{itemize}
        \item \textbf{Failure Analysis}: If a process fails, most operating systems write the error information to a log file.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            The operating system can also take a core dump - a capture of memory of the process and store it in a file. (memory was referred to as the core in early days of computing). Running programs and core dumps can be probed by a debugger.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            A failure in the kernel is called a crash. When a crash occurs, error information is saved to a log file, and the memory state is saved to a crash dump. Operating system debugging, and program debugging frequently use different tools and techniques due to the very different nature of these two tasks.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            A kernel failure in the file-system code would make it unsafe for the kernel to try to save its state to a file on the file system before rebooting. A common technique is to save the kernel’s memory state to a section of disk set aside for this purpose that contains no file system. When the system reboots, a process runs to gather the data from that area and write it to a crash dump file within a file system for analysis.
        \item \textbf{Performance Monitoring and Tuning}:  The operating system must have some means of computing and displaying measures of system behavior.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            Tools may be characterized as providing either \textbf{per-process} or \textbf{system-wide observations.}
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            Tools may use one of two approaches - \textbf{counters or tracing}.
        \item \textbf{Counters}: Operating systems keep track of system activity through a series of counters, such as the number of system calls made or the number of operations performed to a network device or disk.
            \begin{itemize}
                \item \textbf{Per-process}: 
                    \begin{itemize}
                        \item ps - reports information for a single process or selection of processes.
                        \item top - reports real-time statistics for current processes.
                    \end{itemize}
                \item \textbf{System-wide}: 
                    \begin{itemize}
                        \item vmstat - reports memory usage statistics
                        \item netstat - reports statistics for network interfaces
                        \item iostat - reports I/O usage for disks
                    \end{itemize}
            \end{itemize}
            Most counter-based tools on linux systems read statistics from the /proc pseudo file system.
        \item \textbf{Pseudo filesystems}: A pseudo filesystem is a filesystem that does not store data on a physical block device. Instead, it presents kernel-managed data structures as if they were files and directories. It is a filesystem interface layered over in-memory or virtual kernel objects.
        \item \textbf{Tracing}: Whereas counter-based tools simply inquire on the current value of certain statistics that are maintained by the kernel, tracing tools collect data for a specific event - such as the steps involved in a system-call invocation
            \begin{itemize}
                \item \textbf{Per-process}:
                \begin{itemize}
                    \item strace - traces syscalls invoked by a process
                    \item gdb - a source-level debugger
                \end{itemize}
                \item \textbf{System-wide}:
                    \begin{itemize}
                        \item perf - a collection of linux performance tools
                        \item tcpdump - collects network packets
                    \end{itemize}
            \end{itemize}
        \item \textbf{BCC}: A toolkit for dynamic kernel tracing in Linux.
        \item \textbf{ABI}: Hardware-level interface between two pieces of software. Can think of it as a common language that all software that abides by a given ABI speaks
            \begin{itemize}
                \item How to pass arguments to a function
                \item How to pass a function’s return values
                \item How to make system calls.
            \end{itemize}
        \item \textbf{ABI vs API}: API specifies what functionality exists, while the ABI specifies how the functionality interworks using shared hardware resources at the low-level.
            \begin{itemize}
                \item API is high-level and hardware independent.
                \item  ABI is low-level and hardware dependent.
            \end{itemize}
            Programmers focus on APIs, while tool writers (e.g., compiler back-end developers) focus on ABIs. The C compiler enforces the ABI on your behalf.
        \item \textbf{A good definition}: The ABI is a set of rules that compilers and linkers adhere to in order to compile your program so that it will work properly
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            ABIs cover multiple topics
            \begin{itemize}
                \item Arguably the biggest and most important part of an ABI is the \textbf{procedure call standard} sometimes known as the “calling convention". Calling conventions standardize how functions are translated to assembly code.
                \item ABIs also dictate how the names of exposed functions in libraries should be represented so that code can call those libaries and know what arguments should be passed. This is “name mangling".
                \item ABIs also dictate what type of data types can be used, how they must be aligned, and other low-level details.
            \end{itemize}
            An ABI defines low-level binary conventions, including:
            \begin{itemize}
                \item Calling conventions (register usage, stack layout)
                \item System call interface
                \item Object file format (ELF, etc.)
                \item Symbol naming and linkage
                \item Data type sizes and alignment
                \item Exception handling conventions
            \end{itemize}
            It is therefore defined through a combination of:
            \begin{itemize}
                \item Formal specification documents
                \item Compiler conventions
                \item Operating system interfaces
                \item Processor architecture manuals
            \end{itemize}
            It is the contract that ensures independently compiled binaries can interoperate. For each CPU architecture, there is typically a published ABI specification. For x86-64 Linux, the relevant document is: 
            \begin{center}
                \textbf{System V AMD64 ABI}
            \end{center}
            Compilers such as GCC implement the ABI by
            \begin{itemize}
                \item Emitting correct calling sequences
                \item Generating compliant object files (e.g., ELF)
                \item Respecting register and stack conventions
            \end{itemize}
            If a compiler deviates from the ABI, binaries will not link or execute correctly.
        \item \textbf{The core of an ABI: Calling conventions}: The machine itself has no concept of “functions". When you write a function in a high-level language like C, the compiler generates a line of assembly code like \_MyFunction1:. This is a \textit{label}, which will eventually get resolved into an address by the assembler. This label marks the “start" of your “function" in the assembly code. In high-level code When you “call" that function, what you’re really doing is causing the CPU to jump to the address of that label and continue executing there. In preperation for that jump, the compiler must do a bunch of important stuff. The calling convention is like a checklist that the compiler follows to do all this stuff.
            \begin{enumerate}
                \item First, the compiler inserts a little bit of assembly code to save the current address, so that when your “function" is done, the CPU can jump back to the right place and continue executing.
                \item Next the compiler generates assembly code to pass the arguments.
                    \begin{itemize}
                        \item Some calling conventions dictate that arguments should be put on the stack (in a particular order of course).
                        \item still other conventions dictate that a specific combination of stack and registers should be used.
                    \end{itemize}
                \item  If there was anything important in those registers before, those values are now overwritten and lost forever, so some calling conventions may dictate that the compiler should save some of those registers prior to putting the arguments in them.
                \item Now the compiler inserts a jump instruction telling the CPU to go to that label it made previously (\_MyFunction1:). At this point, you can consider the CPU to be “in" your function.
                \item At the end of the function, the compiler puts some assembly code that will make the CPU write the return value in the correct place. The calling convention will dictate whether the return value should be placed into a particular register (depending on its type), or the stack.
                \item Now its time for clean-up. The calling convention will dictate where the compiler places the cleanup assembly code.
                    \begin{itemize}
                        \item Some conventions say that the caller must clean up the stack. This means that after the “function" is done and the CPU jumps back to where it was before, the very next code to be executed should be some very specific cleanup code.
                        \item Other Conventions say that some particular parts of the cleanup code should be at the end of the “function" before the jump back.
                    \end{itemize}
            \end{enumerate}
        \item \textbf{Types of ABIs}: There are many different ABIs / calling conventions. Some main ones are:
            \begin{itemize}
                \item CDECL
                \item STDCALL
                \item FASTCALL
                \item VECTORCALL
                \item THISCALL
            \end{itemize}
        \item \textbf{Where to find the ABI}: ABIs are processor dependent. They are also operating system dependent. They are also possibly tool dependent.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            Using 
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            \begin{bashcode}
            readelf -h bin
            \end{bashcode}
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            on an executable named \texttt{bin} reveals many facts about ELF files on your machine. For example, it might say
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            \begin{bashcode}
                ELF Header:
                Magic:   7f 45 4c 46 02 01 01 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 
                Class:                             ELF64
                Data:                              2's complement, little endian
                Version:                           1 (current)
                OS/ABI:                            UNIX - System V
                ABI Version:                       0
                Type:                              DYN (Position-Independent Executable file)
                Machine:                           Advanced Micro Devices X86-64
                Version:                           0x1
                Entry point address:               0x1050
                Start of program headers:          64 (bytes into file)
                Start of section headers:          13760 (bytes into file)
                Flags:                             0x0
                Size of this header:               64 (bytes)
                Size of program headers:           56 (bytes)
                Number of program headers:         14
                Size of section headers:           64 (bytes)
                Number of section headers:         30
                Section header string table index: 29
            \end{bashcode}
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            So, we see the ABI is System V
        \item \textbf{Linux modules}: When system programmers want to add a new functionality to the linux kernel, they are faced with an interesting dilemma: should they write the new code so that it will be compiled as a module, or should they statically link the new code to the kernel.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            They tend to implement the code as a module, because they can be linked on demand. Nearly every higher-level component of the linux kernel- filesystems, device drivers, executable formats, network layers, etc. can be compiled as a module.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            Some kernel code must be linked statically - it is either compiled with the kernel or not included at all. This typically happens when the component requires a modification to some data structure or function statically linked in the kernel.
        \item \textbf{Static and Dynmaic Linking}: When you statically link a file into an executable, the contents of that file are included at link time.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            when you link dynamically, a pointer to the file being linked in is included in the executable and the contents of said file are not included at link time. It’s only when you run the executable that these dynamically linked files are brought in and they’re only brought into the in-memory copy of the executable, not the one on disk. Its basically a method of deferred linking.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            Statically linked files are ‘locked‘ to the executable at link time so they never change. A dynamically linked file referenced by an executable can change just by replacing the file on the disk.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            Dynamic linking allows modules to be added to the kernel without having to recompile the entire kernel.
        \item \textbf{Virtual memory}: Virtual memory is a memory management technique that provides an "idealized abstraction of the storage resources that are actually available on a given machine" which "creates the illusion to users of a very large (main) memory".
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            The operating system, using a combination of hardware and software, maps memory addresses used by a program, called virtual addresses, into physical addresses in computer memory.
        \item \textbf{A note}: The operating system is not a separate program running “alongside" your program. It is the privileged control layer that schedules and supervises all programs.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            The OS is always running, but not like a normal program. The operating system kernel
            \begin{itemize}
                \item Resides in memory.
                \item Runs in kernel mode (privileged CPU mode).
                \item Gains control via:
                    \begin{itemize}
                        \item Interrupts
                        \item System calls
                        \item Exceptions
                        \item Scheduling events
                \end{itemize}
            \end{itemize}
            User programs run in user mode, which is restricted. The CPU switches between these modes.
    \end{itemize}

    \pagebreak 
    \subsection{Process}
    \begin{itemize}
        \item \textbf{CPU cores}: A CPU core is an independent processing unit within a central processing unit (CPU). Each core is capable of executing program instructions, performing arithmetic and logical operations, managing control flow, and interacting with memory and I/O through the operating system. In architectural terms, a core is a complete execution engine.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            A modern core typically contains:
            \begin{itemize}
                \item \textbf{Control Unit (CU)}: Fetches and decodes instructions.
                \item \textbf{Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU)}: Performs integer arithmetic and logical operations.
                \item \textbf{Floating-Point Unit (FPU)}: - Handles floating-point arithmetic.
                \item \textbf{Registers}: Small, ultra-fast storage (e.g., general-purpose registers, program counter, flags).
                \item \textbf{L1 / L2 Cache}: Private high-speed memory for low-latency access.
                \item \textbf{Branch Predictor}: Predicts control flow to reduce pipeline stalls.
                \item \textbf{Pipeline}: Stages instruction execution (fetch → decode → execute → write-back).
                \item \textbf{Execution Units}: May include SIMD/vector units (e.g., AVX).
            \end{itemize}
        \item \textbf{Intro}: Early computers allowed only one program to be executed at a time. This process has complete control over the system. A process is a program in execution. A process is the unit of work in a modern computer system.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            A system consists of a collection of processes, some executing user code, some executing operating system code. Potentially, all these processes can execute concurrently, with the CPU (or CPUs) multiplexed among them. 
        \item \textbf{Jobs}: Early computer systems executed jobs. We now call them processes. The term jobs have some historical significance. Much of operating system theory and terminology was developed during a time where the major activity of operating systems was job processing.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            Therefore, in some instances we use the term job when describing the role of the operating system. I.e., job scheduling instead of process scheduling.
        \item \textbf{The process}: The status of the current activity of a process is represented by the value of the program counter (PC) and the contents of the processor’s registers. The memory layout of a process is typically divided into multiple sections.
            \begin{itemize}
                \item \textbf{Text section}: the executable code
                \item \textbf{Data section}: global variables
                \item \textbf{Heap section}: memory that is dynamically allocated during program run time.
                \item \textbf{Stack section}: temporary data storage when invoking functions (such as function parameters, return addresses, and local variables)
            \end{itemize}
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            \fig{1}{./figures/1055.png}
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            The stack and heap sections can shrink and grow dynamically during program execution.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            Stack and heap grow towards each other, but the OS makes sure that they do not overlap.
        \item \textbf{Activation records}: Each time a function is called, an activation record containing function parameters, local variables, and the return address is pushed onto the stack. When control is returned from the function, the activation record is popped from the stack.
        \item \textbf{Program vs process}: A program by itself is not a process. A program is a passive entity. A process is an active entity, with a program counter specifying the next instruction to execute and a set of associated resources. A program becomes a process when it is loaded into memory.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            Two processes may be associated with the same program, they are nevertheless considered two separate execution sequences. Example: a user invoking many copies of a web browser program. Each of these are separate processes.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            It is also common to have a process that spawns many processes as it runs.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            A process can itself be an execution environment for other code. Ex. The JVM executes as a process that interprets the loaded java code and takes actions. The command java Program runs the JVM as a process, which in turns executes the Java program Program in the virtual machine.
        \item \textbf{Process state}: As a process executes, it changes state. The state of a process is defined in part by the current activity of that process.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            A process may be in one of the following states:
            \begin{enumerate}
                \item \textbf{New}: The process is being created.
                \item \textbf{Running}: Instructions are being executed.
                \item \textbf{Waiting}: The process is waiting for some event to occur (such as I/O completion or reception of a signal).
                \item \textbf{Ready}: The process is waiting to be assigned to a processor.
                \item \textbf{Terminated}: The process has finished execution.
            \end{enumerate}
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            \fig{.8}{./figures/1056.png}
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            These names are arbitrary and they vary across operating systems. The states that they represent are found on all systems.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            Only one process can be running on any processor core at any instant. Many processes may be ready and waiting, however.
        \item \textbf{Process control block}: Each process is represented in the operating system as a process control block (PCB) - also called a task control block.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            It contains many pieces of information associated with a specific process.
            \begin{itemize}
                \item \textbf{Process state}: The state may be new, ready, running, waiting, halted, and so on.
                \item \textbf{Program counter}: The counter indicates the address of the next instruction to be executed for this process
                \item \textbf{CPU registers}: The registers vary in number and type, depending on the computer architecture. They include accumulators, index registers, stack pointers, and general-purpose registers, plus any condition-code information. Along with the program counter, this state information must be saved when an interupt occurs, to allow the process to be continued correctly afterward when it is rescheduled to run.
                \item \textbf{CPU-scheduling information}: This information includes a process priority, pointers to scheduling queues, and any other sceduling parameters.
                \item \textbf{Memory-management information}: This information may include such items as the value of the base and limit registers and the page tables, or the segment tables, depending on the memory system used by the operating system.
                \item \textbf{Accounting information}: This information includes the amount of CPU and real time used, time limits, account numbers, job or process numbers, and so on.
                \item \textbf{I/O}: status information. This information includes the list of I/O devices allocated to the process, a list of open files, and so on.
            \end{itemize}
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            \fig{1}{./figures/1057.png}
        \item \textbf{Intro to Threads}: The process model discussed so far has implied that a process is a program that performs a single thread of execution.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            A thread is the smallest unit of execution that can be scheduled by an operating system.
            \begin{itemize}
                \item A \textbf{process} is a resource container (address space, file descriptors, heap, etc.).
                \item A \textbf{thread} is a single execution context within that process.
            \end{itemize}
            A thread represents one sequence of instructions with its own control state. An execution context is the complete set of machine-level state required for a CPU to begin or resume executing a sequence of instructions. A thread is the specific computational state that allows code inside a process to actively run.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            A thread has 
            \begin{itemize}
                \item \textbf{Program Counter (PC)}: Address of next instruction
                \item \textbf{Registers}: General-purpose and special-purpose
                \item \textbf{Stack}: Function calls, local variables, return addresses
                \item \textbf{Thread state}: Running, ready, blocked, etc.
            \end{itemize}
            Threads within the same process share:
            \begin{itemize}
                \item Address space
                \item Heap
                \item Global variables
                \item Open files
                \item Memory-mapped regions
            \end{itemize}
            So,
            \begin{center}
                Process = Shared Resources + One or More Threads
            \end{center}
            A single CPU might have 8 cores and 16 threads. Consider the code
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            \begin{cppcode}
            int x = 0;

            void f() { x++; }
            void g() { x--; }

            int main() {
                std::thread t1(f);
                std::thread t2(g);
            }
            \end{cppcode}
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            The process has one address space, and one variable $x$. But, $t1$ has its own execution context, and $t2$ has its own execution context. Each thread has independent register and stack state, but both operate on the same memory. 
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            A CPU core executes one execution context at a time (ignoring SMT). When the OS performs a \textbf{context switch}, it 
            \begin{itemize}
                \item Saves current thread’s registers and PC.
                \item Loads another thread’s registers and PC.
                \item The CPU resumes execution as if uninterrupted.
            \end{itemize}
            So, an execution context is the tuple
            \begin{center}
                (PC, Registers, Stack, Scheduling state)
            \end{center}
            Whereas the process provides
            \begin{center}
                (Address space, Open files, Permissions)
            \end{center}
            Threads separate control flow from resource ownership.
            \begin{itemize}
                \item The process is the building.
                \item The threads are workers inside it.
                \item Each worker has:
                    \begin{itemize}
                        \item Their own clipboard (registers)
                        \item Their own desk (stack)
                        \item Their own position in a task (program counter)
                    \end{itemize}
                \item But they all use the same building resources.
            \end{itemize}
            By default, a standard C++ program uses one thread — the main thread. When you execute a C++ program:
            \begin{itemize}
                \item The OS creates a new process.
                \item The process is initialized with:
                    \begin{itemize}
                        \item One address space
                        \item One stack for the main thread
                        \item One execution context
                    \end{itemize}
                \item Control is transferred to main().
            \end{itemize}
            Consider a word processor program, a single thread of instructions is being executed. This single thread of control allows the process to perform only one task at a time. Thus, the user cannot simultaneously type in characters and run the spell checker.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            Most operating systems have extended the process concept to allow a process to have multiple threads of execution and thus to perform more than one task at a time.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            A multithreaded word processor could, for example, assign one thread to manage user input while another thread runs the spell checker.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            On systems that support threads, the PCB is expanded to include information for each thread.
        \item \textbf{Threads and cores}: On one physical core, at any instant only one hardware execution context can issue instructions (ignoring SMT for the moment). So, on a single-core CPU:
            \begin{align*}
                \text{At any instant } \to \text{ one thread executing}
            .\end{align*}
            Multiple processes are interleaved via context switching. 
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            Say a system has 4 physical cores, no SMT. Then, 4 threads can execute simultaneously, one per core.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            So, system-wide:
            \begin{center}
                Max simultaneous execution = number of cores
            \end{center}
            So the correct statement is:
            \begin{center}
                Only one thread per core executes at any instant.
            \end{center}
            Not:
            \begin{center}
                Only one process executes at a time.
            \end{center}
        \item \textbf{Hardware threads}: A hardware thread is a CPU’s ability to execute an independent instruction stream using a separate architectural state within a single physical core. It is a “slot" inside a CPU core that can run one thread at a time.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            Each hardware thread maintains its own:
            \begin{itemize}
                \item Program counter (instruction pointer)
                \item Register set
                \item Stack pointer
                \item Processor status flags
            \end{itemize}
            This allows multiple threads to make progress without saving/restoring state on every switch.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            \textbf{A hardware thread is the execution context in the CPU where a software thread runs.}
        \item \textbf{SMT (hardware threads)}: Processors from Intel and AMD often implement Simultaneous Multithreading (SMT). If you have 4 physical cores, 2 hardware threads per core, then up to 8 logical threads may execute concurrently. However, 
            \begin{itemize}
                \item Execution units are shared.
                \item It is not double performance.
                \item It improves pipeline utilization.
            \end{itemize}
        \item \textbf{Multiprogramming}: Multiprogramming is an operating system technique that loads multiple programs into main memory simultaneously to maximize CPU utilization, ensuring the processor rarely sits idle. While only one program executes on the CPU at a time, the OS quickly switches between tasks when one waits for I/O operations, creating the illusion of concurrent execution. 
        \item \textbf{Time-sharing}: Time-sharing in computing is a technique where a single computer's resources (like the CPU) are rapidly switched between multiple users or processes, giving each a small "time slice," creating the illusion that they are running concurrently on their own dedicated machine, which makes expensive hardware accessible to many people simultaneously for interactive tasks.
        \item \textbf{Multitasking}: Multitasking is an extension of multiprogramming that uses time-sharing to allow rapid switching between tasks, often for user interaction.
        \item \textbf{Process Scheduling}: The objective of multiprogramming is to have some process running at all times so as to maximize CPU utilization.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            The objective of time sharing is to switch a CPU core among processes so frequently that users can interact with each program while it is running
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            To meet this objectives, the process scheduler selects an available process for program execution on a core. Each CPU core can run one process at a time.
        \item \textbf{Process representation in Linux}: The PCB in Linux is represented by the C struct task\_struct, which is found in include/linux/sched.h in the Linux source code directory.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            Contains the state, scheduling and memory-management information, list of open file, and pointers to the process’s parent and a list of its children and siblings.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            \begin{cppcode}
                long state; /* state of the process */
                struct sched_entity se; /* scheduling information */
                struct task_struct *parent /* the process's parent */
                struct list_head children; /* this process's children */
                struct file_struct *files; /* list of open files */
                struct mm_struct *mm; /* address space */
            \end{cppcode}
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            Within the Linux kernel, all active processes are represented using a doubly-linked-list of task\_struct. The kernel maintains a pointer current to the process currently executing on the system
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            For a system with a single CPU core, there will never be more than one process running at a time, whereas a multicore system can run multiple processes at one time.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            If there are more processes than cores, excess processes will have to wait until a core is free and can be rescheduled.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
        \item \textbf{Degree of multiprogramming}: The number of processes currently waiting in memory is known as the degree of multiprogramming.
        \item \textbf{IO-bound vs CPU-bound processes}: In general most processes can either be described as I/O-bound and CPU-bound. An I/O-bound process spends most of its time doing I/O than it spends doing computations. A CPU-bound process generates I/O requests infrequently, using more of its time doing computations.
        \item \textbf{Scheduling Queues}: As processes enter the system, they are put into a \textbf{ready queue}, where they are ready and waiting to execute on a CPU’s core. This queue is generally stored as a linked list
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            A ready-queue header contains pointers to the first PCB in the list and each PCB includes a pointer field that points to the next PCB in the ready queue.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            The system also includes other queues. When a process is allocated A CPU core, it may wait for the occurrence of a particular event, such as the completion of an I/O request. Suppose the process makes an I/O request to a device such as a disk. Since devices run way slower than processors, the process will have to wait for the I/O to become available. Processes that are waiting for a certain event to occur are placed in a \textbf{wait queue}.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            A common representation of process scheduling is a queueing diagram. Once the process is allocated a CPU core and is executing, one of several events could occur.
            \begin{itemize}
                \item The process could issue an I/O request and then be placed in an I/O wait queue.
                \item The process could create a new child process and then be placed in a wait queue while it awaits the child’s termination.
                \item The process could be removed forcibly from the core, as a result of an interrupt or having its time slice expire, and be put back in the ready queue.
            \end{itemize}
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            \fig{1}{./figures/1058.png}
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            \fc{Queuing-diagram representation of process scheduling. Two types of queues are present: the ready queue and a set of wait queues. The circles represent the resources that serve the queues, and the arrows indicate the flow of processes in the system}
        \item \textbf{CPU Scheduling}: A process migrates among the ready queue and various wait queues throughout its lifetime.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            The role of the \textbf{CPU scheduler} is to select from among the processes that are in the ready queue and allocate a CPU core to one of them.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            The CPU scheduler must select a new process for the CPU frequently. An I/O-bound process may execute for only a few milliseconds before waiting for an I/O request.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            Although a CPU-bound process will require a CPU core for longer durations, the scheduler is unlikely to grant the core to a process for an extended period. Instead, it is likely designed to forcibly remove the CPU from a process and schedule another process to run.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            Therefore, the CPU scheduler executes at least once every 100ms.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
        \item \textbf{Swapping}: Some operating systems have an intermediate form of scheduling, known as swapping. The idea is that sometimes it can be advantageous to remove a process from memory (and from active contention for the CPU) and thus reduce the degree of multiprogramming. The process can be later reintroduced into memory, and its execution can be continued where it left off.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            Swapping is typically only necessary when memory has been overcommitted and must be freed up.
        \item \textbf{Interrupts}: An interrupt is a hardware or software mechanism that forces the CPU to temporarily stop its current execution flow and transfer control to a predefined handler.
            \begin{center}
                Interrupt = Asynchronous control transfer into privileged code
            \end{center}
            Interrupts allow devices to signal: “Something requires attention." For example:
            \begin{itemize}
                \item Keyboard input
                \item Network packet arrival
                \item Disk I/O completion
                \item Timer expiration
            \end{itemize}
            Suppose a timer interrupt fires, the CPU performs
            \begin{enumerate}
                \item Finishes current instruction.
                \item Switches to kernel mode.
                \item Saves:
                \begin{itemize}
                    \item Program counter
                    \item Flags
                    \item Possibly registers
                \end{itemize}
                \item Loads the interrupt handler address from the interrupt descriptor table (IDT).
                \item Executes kernel interrupt handler.
                \item Returns to previous execution context.
            \end{enumerate}
        \item \textbf{Types of interrupts}: 
            \begin{enumerate}
                \item \textbf{Hardware interrupts (external)}: Triggered by external devices
                \item \textbf{Software interrupts}: Triggered intentionally by instructions. Used for system calls.
                    \bigbreak \noindent 
                    These are synchronous
                \item \textbf{Exceptions (Faults / traps) (internal)}: Generated by the CPU itself. These are also interrupt-like control transfers.
            \end{enumerate}
        \item \textbf{Faults, traps, and aborts (exceptions)}: 
            \begin{itemize}
                \item \textbf{Faults}: A fault occurs before the instruction completes. The instruction can usually be retried. Examples are
                    \begin{itemize}
                        \item Page fault
                        \item Divide-by-zero
                        \item General protection fault
                    \end{itemize}
                \item \textbf{Traps}: A trap occurs after the instruction completes. Used intentionally for control transfer. For example syscalls.
                \item \textbf{Aborts}: Serious unrecoverable errors. System often terminates
            \end{itemize}
            Faults and traps are architecturally classified as exceptions, and exceptions are handled through the same mechanism as interrupts.
            \begin{center}
                Interrupts = External asynchronous events \\
                Exceptions (faults/traps) = Internal synchronous events
            \end{center}
            All of them ultimately use the CPU’s interrupt/exception handling machinery.
        \item \textbf{Context switching}:  Interrupts cause the operating system to change a CPU core from its current task and to run a kernel routine.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            When an interrupt occurs the system needs to save the current \textbf{context} of the process running on the CPU core so that it can restore that context when its processing is done, essentially suspending the process and then resuming it.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            The context is represented in the PCB of the process. It includes the value of the CPU registers, the process state, and memory-management information.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            We perform a state save of the current state of the CPU core, be it in kernel or user mode, and then a state restore to resume operations.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            Switching the CPU core to another process requires performing a state save of the current process and a state restore of a different process. This task is known as a context switch.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            When a context switch occurs, the kernel saves the context of the old process in its PCB and loads the saved context of the new process scheduled to run.
        \item \textbf{Process creation}: During the course of execution, a process may create several new processes. The creating process is the parent process, and the new processes are called the children of that process. Each of these new processes may in turn create other processes, forming a tree of processes.
        \item \textbf{Listing processes and the PID}: Many operating systems like Linux identify processes by their process id (pid), which is typically an integer. The pid can be used as an index to access various attributes of a process within the kernel.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            The Systemd process always has a PID of 1 and serves as the root parent process for all user processes, and is the first user process created when the system boots.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            Once the system boots, the Systemd process creates processes which provide additional services like Bluetooth and network managers. On Linux, we can obtain a listing of processes by using the ps command.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            \begin{bashcode}
            ps -el
            \end{bashcode}
            There is also the pstree command, which prints the process tree.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
        \item \textbf{Parent processes and child processes}: When a process creates a child process, that child process will need certain resources (CPU time, memory, files, I/O devices) to accomplish its task. A child may be able to obtain these resources from the operating system or may be constrained to a subset of resources from its parent process.
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            The parent may have to partition its resources among its children, or it may be able to share some resources (such as memory or files). Restricting a child process to a subset of the parent’s resources prevents nay process from overloading the system by creating too many child processes
            \bigbreak \noindent 
            In addition to supplying various physical and logical resources, the parent process may pass along initialization data (input) to the child process.
